Introduction
Disorders affecting the peripheral nervous system may reflect either 
a focal injury to one or more neural structures or a generalized 
neuropathy often associated with other systemic disorders. Focal 
neuropathies are usually the result of localized compression, trac-
tion, or trauma to a specific nerve, whereas generalized neuropa-
thies are typically caused by systemic processes that are often toxic, 
metabolic, or immunologic in nature. With electrophysiologic 
expertise and familiarity with musculoskeletal syndromes, physiat-
rists are positioned to accurately diagnose peripheral nerve disor -
ders and provide rational advice regarding prognosis and therapy. 
An understanding of the anatomic course of nerves and points of 
vulnerability, pathophysiology of nerve disorders, and mechanisms 
of reinnervation enables accurate diagnosis, prognostication, and 
treatment. This chapter introduces principles of nerve anatomy and 
function, pathophysiology, recovery, and prognostication; presents 
common focal peripheral neurologic lesions (focal upper limb, focal 
lower limb, regional nerve disorders, and lower abdominal neu -
ropathies); describes generalized peripheral polyneuropathies; and 
addresses complications of peripheral polyneuropathies and para-
digms in treatment and rehabilitation.
Anatomy and Principles of  
Lesion Localization
The peripheral nervous system is made up of 12 cranial nerves and 
31 spinal nerves that innervate specific sensory distributions 
(dermatomes) and muscle groups (myotomes). In the cervical and 
Lower brachial plexopathies present with hand weakness and 
medial upper limb sensory impairment.
• L umbosacral plexopathies comprise lesions of the lumbar  
or sacral plexuses, with different etiologies and risk factors 
driving each.
• G eneralized peripheral polyneuropathies are characterized by 
pattern and subtype with most peripheral polyneuropathies 
being chronic, length dependent, symmetric, and axonal,  
involving both sensory and motor fascicles. Deviations from 
this pattern should signal consideration of atypical  
neuropathies that may be treatable.
• I nflammatory neuropathies, including Guillain-Barré syndrome 
(acute presentation) and chronic inflammatory demyelinating 
polyradiculoneuropathy with subacute or rapid and chronic 
presentation, are treatable disorders that are important to  
recognize.
• Her editary neuropathies classically present with slowly progres -
sive symptoms, foot deformities, and a predominance of negative 
over positive symptoms and signs. Those representing disorders 
of myelin present with uniform slowing of conduction velocities 
without conduction blocks.
• C omplications of neuropathies include skin breakdown of the 
feet, pain, and functional impairment, including impaired  
balance and falls.
42
Peripheral Nerve Disorders
MONICA BARNES, ANITA CRAIG, AND SANDRA L. HEARN
• T he Seddon and Sunderland classification systems characterize 
focal nerve lesions based on the extent of damage to axons and 
surrounding structures. The pattern (length dependent, single 
nerve, nerve root) and fiber type (motor, sensory, both) guide 
the differential diagnosis.
• Elec trodiagnostic testing can confirm, localize, and characterize 
both focal and generalized neuropathies and establish severity, 
chronicity, and often prognosis. Neuromuscular ultrasound 
complements electrodiagnosis in the assessment of focal and 
generalized neuropathies by evaluating structure.
• D epending on the nature of the lesion, recovery can occur 
through a combination of remyelination, axonal regrowth, and 
collateral sprouting. The recovery process can be serially  
assessed through electrodiagnostic study.
• C ommon compressive mononeuropathies include carpal tunnel 
syndrome, ulnar neuropathy at the elbow and wrist, radial  
neuropathy at the radial groove, fibular neuropathy at the knee,  
lateral femoral cutaneous neuropathy, femoral neuropathy, and 
sciatic neuropathy.
• Br achial plexopathies and regional upper limb nerve disorders 
can be recognized through characteristic sensory and motor 
deficits that span more than one nerve or root but derive from a 
common portion of the plexus. Upper brachial plexopathies 
present with weakness of shoulder movement and elbow  
flexion and sensory impairment along the lateral upper limb.

lumbosacral regions, the spinal nerves intermingle to create plex -
uses, which then form individual peripheral nerves, many of 
which branch further distally in the limb. A peripheral nerve 
branch may carry motor fascicles, sensory fascicles, or both. Thus 
when assessing nerve lesions, the distribution of motor and sen -
sory abnormalities can aid in localization of the lesion within the 
peripheral nervous system to one or more nerves, portions of  
the plexus, or roots. For example, sensation to the fifth digit of the 
hand is supplied by nerve fascicles of the ulnar nerve that, more 
proximally, are part of the medial cord and lower trunk of the 
brachial plexus and the C8 nerve root. Thus a lesion of any of 
these neural structures can cause fifth-digit numbness. Assessment 
of sensory and motor function in other body regions sharing 
some, but not all, of the innervation pathway can help precisely 
localize the lesion.
The Role of Myelin in Impulse Conduction
The basic neural structure is the neuron and its associated axon, 
or nerve fiber. Schwann cells, the glial cells of the peripheral ner -
vous system, encircle one or multiple axons, providing a myelin 
sheath and contributing to speed of action potential conduction 
along an axon. Unmyelinated nerve fibers are groups of axons 
bound by a single Schwann cell. These axons, lacking individual 
myelination, conduct action potentials relatively slowly, at a rate 
of about 1 to 2 m/sec. An example of unmyelinated axons are  
C fibers, which transmit nociceptive input from cutaneous and 
visceral sources. The experience of stubbing one’s toe and feeling 
the pain hit moments later, on a delay, reflects the slow transmis-
sion of the C fibers.
In contrast, myelinated axons each are enwrapped by a series of 
Schwann cells along their lengths, separated by small sections  
of uncovered axon known as nodes of Ranvier ( Fig. 42.1).
79 The 
nodes of Ranvier contain high concentrations of voltage-gated  
sodium channels. Once depolarized, the action potential of a my-
elinated axon “jumps” from node to node in the process of salta-

Connective Tissue Structures and Extent of 
Nerve Injury
Each nerve fiber and its associated Schwann cells are surrounded 
by an endoneurium, which supports and nourishes axons via an 
endoneurial capillary system. Axons are grouped together in fas -
cicles that are enclosed in a perineurium (although individual 
axons can cross from one fascicle to another along the course of 
a nerve). The fascicles are bundled together within the epineu -
rium, which sheaths and nourishes the entire peripheral nerve 
(Fig. 42.2).
105
Focal nerve injuries are classified by the extent of damage to 
the axons and supporting structures. The main classification 
schemes are the Seddon system ( Table 42.1)
146 and the Sunder -
land system ( Table 42.2).161 In the Seddon system, neurapraxia  
refers to conduction failure without axonal loss, as seen with fo -
cal demyelination significant enough to cause conduction block. 
Axonotmesis refers to axonal damage with resultant degeneration 
of the distal component of the axon (wallerian degeneration), 
while the supporting connective tissue structures, including 
outer epineurium, remain intact. Neurotmesis, the most extensive 
category of nerve injury, refers to complete disruption of the 
axons and connective tissue structures, as with complete nerve 
transection.
The Sunderland system further delineates the degree of connec-
tive tissue damage by specific layer (see Table 42.2). First-degree 
injury corresponds to Seddon neurapraxia, and the higher degrees

the compound muscle action potential (CMAP) and conduction 
velocity (CV) will be normal when stimulating distal to the lesion, 
as the motor axon remains intact, while stimulation proximal to 
the lesion will yield a CMAP with reduced amplitude (partial 
neurapraxia) or no response (complete neurapraxia). Needle elec-
tromyography (EMG) will show absent or decreased motor unit 
action potential (MUAP) recruitment commensurate with the 
proportion of axons blocked. Positive waves and fibrillation poten-
tials are not seen, nor are changes in MUAP morphology because 
there is no motor axon loss.
In an axonal (rather than neurapraxic) lesion, the same pattern 
is observed in the initial days postinjury. This phase is known as 
hyperacute axonal loss, wherein the axonal segment distal to the 
lesion has not yet undergone wallerian degeneration. Specifically, 
sensory nerve action potential (SNAP) and CMAP amplitudes are 
normal when stimulating the nerve distal to the lesion and abnor-
mal when stimulating proximally. On EMG, motor unit recruit-
ment is decreased commensurate with the quantity of axons lost.
Wallerian degeneration spreads distally from the site of the  
lesion and occurs over a period of about 7 days for motor axons and 
about 10 days for sensory axons. Once these events have trans -
pired, the relevant SNAP and CMAP amplitudes are low regard -
less of stimulation site. Muscle membrane instability, manifesting 
as denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves), 
becomes evident around 3 weeks after injury, with abnormalities 
first noted proximally (closer to the lesion) and progressing  
distally over time.
139 It is important to note that fibrillation  
potentials may also be seen in direct muscle trauma, which can 
complicate localization of nerve injury.
Recovery of focal nerve injury occurs by three mechanisms 
depending on the nature and severity of injury: resolution of 
neurapraxia, collateral sprouting, and axonal regeneration.
139 
Resolution of neurapraxia occurs rapidly, with remyelination 
usually complete by 3 months, although more severe or chronic 
lesions may take longer. In contrast, reinnervation after axonal 
loss relies on collateral sprouting, axonal regrowth, or a combina-
tion of both mechanisms. These mechanisms are illustrated in 
Fig. 42.4 and described here.
Collateral sprouting is seen in partial axonal lesions, wherein 
intact axons sprout new terminal nerve branches to reinnervate 
“orphaned” muscle fibers (see Fig. 42.4A). An axon can reinner -
vate approximately up to five times its original number of muscle

represent axonal injury with varying degrees of damage to connec-
tive tissue structures. Higher degrees of injury carry poorer  
prognoses for spontaneous recovery, with neurotmesis and/or fifth-
degree injury having no capacity for spontaneous axonal regrowth, 
due to lack of connective tissue pathways to the distal muscle or 
cutaneous territory. Notably, electrodiagnostic testing (EDX) can 
evaluate axonal continuity but cannot evaluate integrity of the sup-
portive tissue structures.
Nerve Injury, Recovery, and  
Electrodiagnostic Assessment
EDX is particularly helpful in confirming and localizing focal 
neuropathies. Additionally, it provides critical information about 
the severity and chronicity of the injury and can be useful in de -
termining the prognosis of the injury. The expected evolution of 
electrophysiologic abnormalities differs for primarily neurapraxic 
vs. axonal lesions (Fig. 42.3).
Until about 1 week postinjury, neurapraxia and axonal loss are 
electrophysiologically indistinguishable. In purely neurapraxic in-
juries, in which there is conduction block without axonal damage,

fibers, aligning with the observation that injuries with loss of less 
than about 70% to 80% of axons typically show good recovery 
with conservative management.
15 Collateral sprouting takes about 
2 to 3 months to manifest on EMG, and it is recognized through 
morphologic changes of the MUAPs. With early reinnervation, 
MUAPs show increased polyphasia, duration, and motor unit 
instability caused by desynchrony of the muscle fiber action po-
tentials within the MUAP in the setting of immature neuromus -
cular junctions and more slowly conducting terminal nerve 
branches with newly forming myelin. As these new components 
mature, their electrophysiologic signal becomes assimilated into 
the primary MUAP , yielding waveforms with increased duration 
and amplitude (less polyphasia). Very remote and mature collat -
eral sprouting manifests as triphasic MUAPs with significantly 
increased amplitude and only modestly increased duration.
Axonal regrowth represents the third mechanism of recovery. 
In partial axonal lesions, it occurs in conjunction with collateral 
sprouting, especially where there has been extensive axonal loss. 
Complete lesions with no axonal continuity to muscle rely en -
tirely on axonal regrowth for reinnervation. The proximal nerve 
stump begins to sprout within 24 to 48 hours after injury and 
grows distally (see Fig. 42.4B). Recovery depends on degree of 
injury (including damage to supporting connective tissue struc -
tures), intervening scar tissue, and distance from target muscle. 
Regeneration occurs at a rate of 1 to 5 mm/day and progresses 
in a proximal to distal fashion, with the earliest signs seen in 
muscles just distal to the site of injury.
162 On EMG, these initial 
MUAPs show low amplitude, increased polyphasia, and de-
creased recruitment.
Prognostication
Rendering a prognosis after peripheral nerve lesions i s complex 
and depends on an array of factors. Purely neurapraxic nerve in -
juries have very good prognosis, with recovery in a few months, if 
the cause of compression is eliminated, although conduction 
slowing may persist on nerve conduction studies. Recovery of 
mixed demyelinating and axonal injury is usually biphasic, with 
demyelinating components resolving in a few months and axonal 
recovery proceeding more slowly. The prognosis of axonal nerve 
lesions depends on the degree of loss and can be estimated by the 
reduction of CMAP amplitude compared with the opposite side. 
In studies of facial nerve lesions, those with CMAP amplitude 
.30% of the opposite side had a very good prognosis, while those 
with less than 10% had a poor outcome.
153
Prognosis is more challenging for cases of severe axonal loss, 
with extent of axonal loss exceeding about 70%, including for 
complete lesions with no evidence of axonal continuity. Complete 
lesions rely on axonal regrowth (rather than collateral sprouting or 
remyelination). Capacity for axonal regrowth, in turn, depends on 
viability of connective tissue scaffolding, which is not directly  
assessed with electrophysiologic testing. On the one extreme, com-
plete neurotmesis has no capacity to recover without surgical inter-
vention; two severed ends of the nerve will retract, and there is no 
capacity for regrowth across a gap. When such lesions can be 
readily identified clinically (e.g., open wound with nerve lacera -
tion) or by imaging, surgical repair is typically indicated. In other 
cases, EDX testing may determine that a lesion is complete with 
no axonal continuity beyond the site of injury, but no information

can be derived about the viability of the supporting connective 
tissue elements. In such cases, repeat EDX may be considered to 
assess for evidence of early reinnervation by axonal regrowth. Serial 
EDX may thus guide surgical decision making, when performed  
at intervals up until the time of a surgical decision (typically  
6 to 9 months postinjury, to allow sufficient time for axonal re -
growth after the surgical intervention).
66 Because the distal muscle 
fibers and connective tissue structures remain viable for reinnerva-
tion for only about 18 to 24 months postdenervation, capacity for 
reinnervation is time limited and distance dependent.
Factors influencing prognosis of nerve lesions are depicted in 
Fig. 42.5. Aside from the neuroanatomic and neurophysiologic 
factors discussed earlier, assessment of prognosis should  
account for the underlying clinical process: whether the lesion 
represents an isolated event vs. a progressive process, and 
whether the pathophysiologic mechanism likely disrupts sup -
portive tissue structures (e.g., laceration) vs. axon alone (e.g., 
immune-mediated neuropathy).
Ultrasound in Diagnosis of Peripheral Nerve 
Injury
The role of ultrasound in the diagnosis of peripheral nerve injuries 
has been growing. Ultrasound can complement EDX in diagnos-
ing focal nerve injury and generalized neuropathies. In the setting 
of acute trauma, such as motor vehicle accidents, ultrasound may 
be able to localize sites of nerve trauma that are not accessible to 
nerve conduction study. As signs of denervation are not seen in 
muscle until wallerian degeneration occurs 3 to 4 weeks after 
trauma, needle EMG cannot identify axonal injury in the early 
postinjury phase, but ultrasound can demonstrate structural find-
ings such as focal nerve compression or even complete nerve dis -
continuity, which may guide the immediate treatment approach. 
In some cases, the morphology of the injured nerve on ultrasound 
may also help predict prognosis for recovery. Coraci et al. showed 
that enlargement of the fibular nerve greater than 300% of nor -
mal on ultrasound had no recovery of function.
26 In nontrau-
matic peripheral nerve injuries, ultrasound can provide additional 
structural information. Ultrasound may find abnormality when 
EDX studies are normal and can be helpful for assessment after 
surgical intervention as EDX abnormalities can persist even after 
clinical improvement. The role of ultrasound in individual focal 
neuropathies will be discussed further.
67
Neuromuscular ultrasound may also assist in the diagnosis of 
generalized peripheral neuropathies. Those with acquired peripheral 
neuropathies, such as chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyra-
diculoneuropathy (CIDP), may have multifocal enlargement of  
peripheral nerves, fascicular enlargement, and increased nerve vascu-
larity. Hereditary demyelinating neuropathies can be distinguished 
from acquired conditions, as hereditary demyelinating disorders will 
have diffuse and symmetric nerve enlargement. Ultrasound is also 
relevant in the diagnosis of multifocal motor neuropathy and will 
show regional nerve enlargement. This finding can help differentiate 
this condition from motor neuron disease, in which nerves will have 
normal or decreased cross-sectional area.
11
Focal Peripheral Nerve Injuries
T raumatic focal nerve injuries are usually caused by laceration, 
compression, or traction of a specific nerve, often in a location of 
anatomic vulnerability (superficial location, adjacency to bone, 
passage through a tight canal, change in trajectory along a long 
course).
65 They can occur after repetitive motion, direct forceful 
trauma, or prolonged compression caused by positioning or use of 
bracing or assistive devices; they can arise from a single injury or 
from repeated biomechanical stress over time. An understanding 
of the anatomic distribution of specific nerves, their course and 
areas of mechanical vulnerability, and the specific muscles and 
sensory distribution they supply is critical in localizing the  
patient’s lesion and formulating strategies for treatment.
Notably, focal neuropathies often accompany multitraumatic 
events and may be initially overlooked in the setting of cata -
strophic traumatic injury. Life-threatening injury, major trauma to 
bone and tissue, and altered mental status may delay recognition 
of a serious nerve injury that later impedes expected functional 
progress. Up to 10% to 34% of patients admitted to rehabilitation 
units with traumatic brain injury have peripheral nerve injuries.
28 
It is therefore important to be vigilant for these injuries in the  
appropriate clinical setting.
The following sections address common focal nerve lesions of 
the upper and lower limbs.
Focal Neuropathies of the Upper Limb
Median Mononeuropathies
The medial and lateral cords of the brachial plexus form the me -
dian nerve just distal to the level of the axilla. The nerve courses 
parallel to the intermuscular septum and then passes under the  
bicipital aponeurosis before entering the antecubital fossa medial 
to the biceps tendon and anterior to the brachialis muscles.  
In 1% to 2% of the population, there is a small bone spur,

called the ligament of Struthers, which crosses over the nerve, and

163 The median nerve then 
courses between the superficial and deep heads of the pronator 
teres and then between the humeroulnar and radial portions of 
the flexor digitorum superficialis, supplying these muscles along 
with the flexor carpi radialis. Distal to the medial epicondyle of 
the humerus, the median nerve gives off the anterior interosseous 
nerve (AIN), which supplies the lateral portions of the flexor 
digitorum profundus, flexor pollicis longus, and pronator qua -
dratus muscles. Proximal to the wrist, the median nerve gives off  
the palmar cutaneous branch, supplying sensation to the skin 
over the thenar eminence. At the wrist, the median nerve passes 
through the carpal canal, which is bordered by the carpal bones 
and the transverse carpal ligament. The carpal canal contains 
nine flexor tendons (flexor digitorum superficialis [four], flexor 
digitorum profundus [four], and flexor pollicis longus) and the 
median nerve (Fig. 42.6).
107 Distal to the carpal tunnel in the 
hand, the median nerve supplies the first and second lumbricals, 
opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, superficial portion of 
flexor pollicis brevis, and digital cutaneous branches, which pro -
vide sensation to the palmar surface of the thumb and second, 
third, and lateral portion of the fourth fingers (Fig. 42.7).  
The median nerve is most vulnerable to injury at the level of the 
carpal tunnel.
Median Mononeuropathy at the WRIST  
(Carpal Tunnel Syndrome)
Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is the most common compressive 
neuropathy. In CTS, the median nerve is compressed within the 
carpal canal, approximately 1 to 2 cm beyond the distal wrist 
crease. It is more common in females and is typically more severe 
in the dominant hand, but is frequently bilateral.
152 Most cases 
are idiopathic, but some risk factors have emerged. Repetitive 
hand and wrist movements, occupations requiring high grip 
force, and the use of vibratory tools have been associated with 
CTS.
65 Medical conditions associated with CTS include diabetes, 
hypothyroidism, and rheumatoid arthritis (RA),159 as well as obe-
sity12 and pregnancy. Infrequently, CTS can be caused by a mass

CTS typically presents with paresthesias and numbness of the 
second and third digits and variably the thumb and lateral fourth 
digits, although often the patient will have more diffuse com -
plaints of the entire hand being numb. Sensory supply to the the-
nar eminence is spared, as the palmar cutaneous branch of the 
median nerve arises proximal to the carpal tunnel and courses 
outside it. Classically, in the early phases, symptoms are intermit-
tent, often worse at night, causing nocturnal awakening, and im -
proved by flicking the hands and placing them in a dependent 
position. Other aggravating activities include driving or grasping 
objects, such as a telephone, book, or steering wheel, with a flexed 
or extended wrist and with activation of the finger flexors. Patients 
may describe pain or discomfort that radiates proximally through 
the forearm or even to the shoulder. Provocative maneuvers, such 
as a Tinel or Phalen sign (Fig. 42.8), may reproduce symptoms in 
the median nerve distribution, but should be interpreted in the 
appropriate clinical context because they may exacerbate other 
etiologies of hand and wrist pain. Muscle stretch reflexes will be 
unaffected by CTS.
More advanced cases with axonal loss may manifest a fixed 
sensory deficit of the median innervated digits and hand weakness 
(lateral lumbricals, abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, and 
a portion of flexor pollicis brevis), manifesting as difficulty with 
opening jars and manipulating buttons. Video 42.1 demonstrates 
weakness of left thumb abduction. Atrophy of the thenar emi -
nence can be seen when the injury is severe and chronic. Long 
finger flexor strength should be normal, as should ulnar-innervated 
finger abduction. The differential diagnosis of CTS includes

thy. Clinical findings suggesting an alternative diagnosis include  
weakness of non–median innervated muscles or proximal median 
innervated muscles, asymmetry of muscle stretch reflexes, or 
prominent neck pain. Electrodiagnostic studies can confirm the 
diagnosis of CTS and assess the severity, as well as exclude other 
neurologic diagnoses. EDX has a sensitivity of 80% to 90% for 
CTS. Median sensory latencies are prolonged in mild disease and 
are compared with the ulnar sensory latency in the same hand. 
Midpalmar stimulation might be more sensitive because it records 
over a shorter segment across the carpal tunnel and contains the 
larger diameter sensory fascicles that supply the muscle spindles, 
which are more vulnerable to demyelination.
38 Median motor re-
sponses are affected in moderate disease and will show decreased 
compound muscle action potential amplitude and prolonged dis-
tal latency, with normal CV across the forearm. Severe CTS will 
show signs of axonal loss, such denervation or reinnervation of 
abductor pollicis brevis or opponens pollicis muscles.
Ultrasound complements EDX testing in evaluating CTS and 
may show abnormalities in patients with clinical symptoms of 
CTS but normal EDX studies. Common abnormalities seen on 
ultrasound include enlargement of the median nerve, flattening of 
the nerve in the carpal canal, bowing of the flexor retinaculum, 
increased intraneural vascularity, and decreased median nerve 
mobility with wrist flexion. A cross-sectional area of greater than 
9 mm
2 is the best criteria for CTS with a sensitivity and specificity 
of 71% and 86%, respectively.164 Ultrasound can show structural 
abnormalities that may impact treatment and surgical manage -
ment and may sometimes elucidate structural etiologies for persis-
tent symptoms after carpal tunnel release.81
Mild to moderate CTS usually responds to conservative mea -
sures. Exacerbating activities, such as repetitive or excessive wrist 
flexion and extension and gripping, should be avoided. Wrist 
splints are a safe and effective treatment. The wrist should be 
splinted in 0 to 5 degrees of extension at night and as much as 
tolerated during the day. Nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs 
(NSAIDs) are often used, but evidence is limited; a short course 
of oral steroids may be helpful.
22 Local corticosteroid injections 
into the carpal tunnel can improve symptoms as well as nerve 
function (based on electrodiagnostic studies) and are effective for 
at least 3 months.
8,62 T wo injections may be required if nocturnal 
paresthesia persists after the initial treatment.97 In addition to the 
antiinflammatory and analgesic properties, steroids may also act

mechanism in CTS is not confirmed. Surgical decompression is 
indicated in patients with severe or rapidly progressive weakness 
and atrophy, for those who fail to respond to conservative mea -
sures, or if there is a mass lesion causing the median nerve com -

Proximal Median Nerve Injuries
Compression of the median nerve in the forearm is much less 
common than compression distally at the carpal canal (CTS). The 
primary levels of compression in the forearm are at the AIN and 
at the pronator muscle.
The AIN can be compressed by the fibrous arch formed by the 
flexor digitorum superficialis and the pronator teres muscles. The 
nerve can also be injured from repetitive forearm flexion or prona-
tion, elbow or forearm fractures, or associated with neuralgic 
amyotrophy. As the AIN is a purely motor branch, this injury is a 
purely motor syndrome with weakness of flexion of the interpha-
langeal joint of the thumb and distal interphalangeal joints of the 
index and middle fingers. Accordingly, the patient will be unable 
to form the “OK” sign and will substitute with a key pinch 
(Fig. 42.9). Pronation may weaken due to involvement of the 
pronator quadratus, although it is less apparent because of intact 
strength of the pronator teres, which is spared. T esting pronation 
with the elbow in flexion will better isolate the action of the 
pronator quadratus. Although there is no sensory loss, patients 
may experience an aching pain in the forearm. On electrodiagnos-
tic studies, standard nerve conduction studies are normal, and 
needle examination will demonstrate denervation in the muscles 
supplied by the AIN.
Reports of improvement within 3 to 24 months from symp -
tom onset
54 suggests the etiology is often inflammatory (e.g., 
neuralgic amyotrophy) rather than mechanical compression. It is 
therefore recommended that unless there is an identified compres-
sive lesion, surgical exploration and decompression should be 
delayed unless there is no recovery after 12 months.
140
The median nerve may also be compressed as it passes between 
the two heads of the pronator teres (pronator syndrome [PS]), a 
fascial band from the flexor digitorum superficialis, or the biceps 
aponeurosis. In cases of PS, all median innervated muscles, aside 
from the pronator teres, will be affected, as well as the entire

Electrodiagnostic studies may show median sensory ampli-

median-innervated muscles except pronator teres.
Conservative treatment of PS is usually initiated with a trial of 
NSAIDs, physical therapy, and avoidance of repetitive elbow flex-

be applied. Surgical decompression can be indicated if there is no

Ulnar Mononeuropathies
The ulnar nerve derives from the medial cord, lower trunk, and 
C8 and T1 roots. It courses through the medial arm in close prox-
imity to the median nerve. In the elbow region, there are two 
main sites of vulnerability to injury ( Fig. 42.10). The first is the 
ulnar (retroepicondylar) groove, a depression between the medial 
epicondyle and the olecranon in which the nerve courses superfi -
cially. The second is at the humeroulnar aponeurosis, where the 
nerve courses deep to the Osborne ligament and enters the space 
between the two heads of the flexor carpi ulnaris. At each of these

the left side regardless of hand dominance; in addition to 
mechanically demanding events, such as repetitive elbow flexion, 
gripping, or external pressure over the ulnar groove,

Patients with UNE will typically present with dysesthesias and 
sensory changes in the ulnar distribution of the hand. Patients  
report very well-circumscribed numbness in the ulnar half of the 
fourth digit and the entire fifth digit, as opposed to the more vari-
able and sometimes whole hand numbness seen with CTS. Patients 
may also report pain or discomfort over the medial aspect of the 
forearm along the course of the ulnar nerve; however, sensation in 
this region is spared because it is innervated by the medial ante-
brachial cutaneous nerve. In more advanced disease, patients report 
loss of dexterity and grip strength, affecting functional activities.
Physical examination can reveal altered sensation in the ulnar 
distribution of the hand, especially if there has been axonal loss. 
A Tinel sign is often elicited over the nerve in the ulnar groove 
and/or cubital tunnel. Strength of the interossei may be dimin -
ished, and with advanced cases, atrophy of the region over the first 
dorsal interosseous muscle is noted (often in association with 
some “clawing” of the hand caused by intrinsic muscle weakness).
Electrodiagnostic studies may be useful in localizing UNE to 
the elbow and assessing severity, as well as ruling out other neuro-
logic causes. Reported sensitivity of EDX testing ranges from 37% 
to 86% depending on the technique and gold standard used.
68 
Nerve conduction studies should assess for focal slowing or con-
duction block of ulnar motor fascicles across the elbow. Secondary 
axonal loss may result in decreased amplitude of SNAP and 
CMAP responses. In severe disease, axon loss may predominate, 
causing a decrease in CMAP amplitude across all sites of stimula-
tion, making lesion localization challenging. The first dorsal inter-
osseous is the muscle most likely affected during EMG needle  
examination, with the abductor digit minimi and flexor carpi  
ulnaris, respectively, less involved. C8-T1 radiculopathy and lower 
trunk plexopathy can be differentiated from UNE through the 
finding of EMG abnormalities in nonulnar innervated muscles 
(e.g., opponens pollicis, flexor pollicis longus, and extensor indi -
cis). Lower brachial plexopathy typically shows a low medial ante-
brachial cutaneous SNAP amplitude; radiculopathy may also show 
abnormalities in the paraspinal muscles.
In patients with clinical symptoms of UNE and normal EDX 
studies, ultrasound can be diagnostic. Ultrasound testing demon-
strated an 80.5% sensitivity, with UNE present with cross-sectional 
area of greater than 9.8 mm
2 at the retroepicondylar groove.42 Ul-
trasound may be particularly valuable in localizing the site of nerve 
compression and be complementary to short segment nerve con -
duction studies. Ultrasound and EDX localization concurred in 
almost 50% of patients and was able to solely identify the site of 
compression in a quarter of patients in one study.
115 Ultrasound 
also allows for dynamic testing, showing ulnar nerve subluxation or 
dynamic impingement of the triceps brachii muscle with elbow 
motion. In those with demonstrated subluxation, nerve transposi-
tion may be preferred during surgical decompression.
The results of treatment for UNE, both surgical and nonsurgi-
cal, are variable. Strategies include avoidance of strong, repetitive 
gripping, preventing repetitive or prolonged elbow flexion (using 
a splint as necessary), cushioning of the elbow to prevent com -
pression over the ulnar nerve, smoking cessation if warranted, and 
patient education. As smoking appears to be an independent risk 
factor for UNE and reduces patient satisfaction after surgery, 
avoidance of tobacco is a reasonable recommendation.
135 Absent 
clinical or electrodiagnostic improvement within 2 to 3 months, 
surgical evaluation for possible decompression or transposition of 
the nerve may be considered.
Ulnar Neuropathy at the Wrist
Ulnar neuropathy at the wrist (UNW) is much less common than 
UNE and CTS and is typically caused by direct compression. Re-
petitive and forceful gripping of a tool that applies pressure over the 
medial palm is a classic mechanism. Four potential sites of injury 
within Guyon canal give rise to four distinct clinical presentations 
with varying degrees of sensory and motor involvement (Fig. 42.12).

specific fascicles increases sensitivity for this lesion compared with 
recording abductor digiti minimi alone, given the high propor -

study, whose fascicles are spared with UNW , is sometimes used; 
however, it is sometimes spared with UNE
108,180 and, conversely, 
can be unobtainable in some individuals without pathology due

108 One should exercise similar caution with 
localizing a lesion to the wrist based solely on EMG abnormali -

with UNE. Ultrasound can identify nerve enlargement, hy-
poechogenicity, and a loss of normal fascicular pattern. Space-
occupying lesions within the canal may also be identified easily 
under ultrasound.
20 Initial conservative management is appropri-

eliminated. Cases with a severe and progressive motor deficit or 
with an anatomic cause unlikely to improve with activity 
modification (mass or ganglion cyst) may benefit from surgical 
decompression.
Radial Mononeuropathies
The radial nerve is the largest terminal branch off the brachial 
plexus, with contributions from C5 to C8 nerve roots. Arising 
from the posterior cord of the plexus, it runs along the lateral wall 
of the axilla, where it is first susceptible to compression. It then 
supplies the triceps brachii, and then winds around the radial 
groove of the humerus, where it is again susceptible to injury. It 
pierces the intermuscular septum below the deltoid and supplies 
the brachioradialis muscle and extensor carpi radialis longus 
above the elbow. The nerve enters the forearm between the biceps 
and brachioradialis muscles and gives off the superficial radial 
nerve at the level of the elbow joint. It then travels around the 
neck of the radius, passing through the supinator (arcade of 
Frohse), as the deep radial motor branch. After innervating the 
supinator and extensor carpi radialis brevis, the nerve emerges as 
the posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) and enters the extensor 
compartment of the forearm. Here it supplies the extensor digito-
rum, extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor pollicis longus and brevis, 
abductor pollicis longus, extensor indicis, and extensor digiti 
minimi. The superficial radial nerve traverses the forearm to sup -
ply sensation to the dorsolateral portion of the hand and first 
three digits (Fig. 42.13). 
The radial nerve is less susceptible to compression than the me-
dian and ulnar nerves. The most common lesion sites are the radial 
groove and the arcade of Frohse (PIN). Occasionally, the superficial 
radial branch will be injured at the wrist, causing purely sensory 
abnormalities on the dorsolateral aspect of the hand. This is encoun-
tered with use of handcuffs, tight wristwatches, or even from using 
wrist braces for other conditions, such as CTS.
Radial Neuropathy at the Radial Groove
The radial nerve is most commonly injured at the radial groove 
of the humerus as a result of insults, including humeral fractures, 
tourniquets, or compression during prolonged sleep or uncon -
sciousness. In contrast to direct compression, a different mecha -
nism of injury—intense muscular contraction of the triceps—
can lesion the nerve slightly more distally at the tendinous 
insertion of the lateral head of the triceps brachii. Regardless of 
mechanism, radial mononeuropathy in the arm presents with 
wrist drop and inability to extend the fingers. Elbow extension is 
usually spared as the triceps is innervated proximal to the radial 
groove. Slight weakness of elbow flexion may result from involve-
ment of the brachioradialis. Median and ulnar innervated muscle 
strength will be unaffected; however, finger abduction can falsely 
appear weak unless the metacarpophalangeal joints of the hand 
are held in passive extension. Sensation is abnormal over the 
dorsolateral hand and the dorsum of digits 1 through 4. The 
brachioradialis reflex is diminished or absent, whereas the triceps 
and biceps reflexes are spared.
EDX can aid in localization and in exclusion of differential 
diagnoses, which may include radiculopathy or posterior cord  
lesions.
Management of proximal radial nerve injuries depends on 
the etiology of the injury. External compression typically 
causes neurapraxic injury that usually recovers spontaneously 
within 2 months.
170 Poorer outcomes are seen with humeral 
fracture. If any response can be recorded from the extensor 
indicis on nerve conduction studies, there is a 90% chance of 
recovering at least antigravity wrist strength.
98 If there is no 
recovery within 8 to 10 weeks, surgical exploration is indi -
cated, and if there is no return of function after 1 year, tendon 
transfer may be considered to improve upper limb function -
ing. During the recovery period, a cock-up splint is used to 
maintain the wrist and fingers in an extended position to pre -
vent contracture.

Disorders of Shoulder Abduction
Consideration of nerve disorders affecting shoulder function 
should include assessment of the axillary and suprascapular 
nerves.
Axillary nerve. The axillary nerve branches from the posterior 
cord. It traverses through the quadrilateral space to innervate the 
deltoid and teres minor muscles and provides cutaneous innerva -
tion to a region overlying the lateral proximal arm. It can be in -
jured in the quadrilateral space (Fig. 42.14),
13 typically in the 
context of trauma, such as glenohumeral dislocation or direct 
impact. Axillary mononeuropathy presents with weakness of

Suprascapular nerve. The suprascapular nerve is most suscep-
tible to injury at or between the suprascapular and spinoglenoid 
notches (suprascapular canal).
4 At the suprascapular notch, a 
small foramen in the superior scapula bordered superiorly by the

130 the nerve angles sharply within its

activities with this mechanism include performing an overhead 
spike during volleyball or heaving loads over the shoulder.

Suprascapular mononeuropathy can present as shoulder pain and/
or weakness of external rotation and abduction.
Suprascapular nerve injury localizing to the spinoglenoid 
notch can yield isolated weakness of infraspinatus and may result 
from shoulder joint pathology, such as a paralabral cyst compress-
ing the nerve.
Initial management is typically conservative, involving exer -
cises to strengthen the shoulder girdle, utilizing other tools to 
minimize shoulder loading during occupational tasks, and sus -
pension of repetitive strain activities.
Disorders of Scapular Movement
Nerve lesions affecting innervation to the scapular stabilizers can 
lead to scapular winging. Assessment of scapular winging re -
quires a careful physical exam, with sufficient clothing removed 
to observe the scapulae at rest and dynamically. The scapulae 
should be examined through the ranges of motion (ROM) of 
shoulder flexion and abduction, as the specific nerve lesion drives 
the dynamic pattern of weakness. Evaluation of scapular mobility 
functionally through a modified pushup or wall pushup can of -
ten differentiate voluntary scapular winging from a neurologic 
deficit. Specifically, some individuals can voluntarily produce 
scapular winging, absent neurologic damage, which can be a 
point of confusion for patients and practitioners alike during the 
physical examination; assessment for natural dyskinesis through 
coordinated, composite movement such as a pushup can help 
draw the distinction.
150

dyskinesis.100
Other Diagnostic and Management Considerations
In assessing nerve lesions affecting the shoulder region, neural -
gic amyotrophy often presents as mononeuropathy or multiple 
mononeuropathies of the above nerves. This entity is discussed 
in Regional Nerve Disorders, later. Early recognition guides 
management.
T reatment of these mononeuropathies largely depends on eti-
ology and extent of axonal loss. Potentially progressive lesions 
(e.g., nerve compression by mass) require medical or surgical 
treatment of underlying cause. For static lesions with significant 
axonal loss (substantial weakness or .70% axonal loss), surgical 
consultation may be indicated, and serial EDX may be considered 
to assess for early recovery. Mild lesions can be managed conser -
vatively.

The brachial plexus is susceptible to direct trauma, including 
but not limited to birth injury. T rauma may affect multiple  
regions of the plexus, and assessment for plexopathy should be 
considered in the setting of clavicle fracture.
Electrodiagnostic studies of the affected limb show sensory and 
motor abnormalities in the distribution of the affected portions of 
the plexus. Effort should be made to obtain a reliable sensory  
response originating from each of the major components of the 
plexus, as SNAP abnormalities distinguish plexopathy from  
radiculopathy. In the context of regional trauma, a combination of 
normal SNAPs despite dense numbness and poor or absent motor 
function indicates preganglionic injury and raises suspicion for 
nerve root avulsion, which carries a poor prognosis for spontane -
ous recovery. Needle EMG should sample muscles representing 
each portion of the plexus (trunks, cords, and peripheral nerves) 
to localize the level of the lesion and to determine axonal continu-
ity (Table 42.4). Imaging studies may be indicated to evaluate for 
underlying tumor, mass, or disruption of the plexus. Ultrasound 
can sometimes identify root avulsion and allow for an earlier 
decision on surgical intervention.

Rehabilitation of brachial plexopathy includes proper posi -
tioning with orthoses to stabilize affected joints and improve 
function. ROM exercises and physiotherapy are important to 
reduce contractures and strengthen recovering motor function. 
Medication management is appropriate for neuropathic pain. 
Surgical treatment can consist of neurolysis or primary nerve 
repair. Nerve grafting, nerve transfers, or tendon transfers can 
be used if there is no improvement or if there is a plateau in 
recovery.
Upper Brachial Plexopathies
Lesions of the upper brachial plexus (C5 and C6 anterior primary 
rami and upper trunk) manifest clinically as weakness of shoulder 
abduction, elbow flexion, and forearm supination, also known as 
Erb palsy.
The upper trunk is particularly vulnerable when the head and 
neck are forcibly bent away from the shoulder, causing traction 
of the upper brachial plexus. Milder plexus injuries have been 
associated with sports, particularly American football. So-called 
stingers or burners are caused by a blow to the head, neck, or 
shoulder and cause a sharp burning pain in the shoulder with 
transient weakness.
Lower Brachial Plexopathies
Lesions of the lower brachial plexus (C8 and T1 anterior pri-
mary rami and lower trunk) produce weakness and atrophy of 
hand intrinsic muscles and sensory deficits along the medial 
upper limb. This can be a result of a space-occupying lesion, 
such as a mass, injury during operative procedures, such as a 
median sternotomy, or they can occur as the trunk passes 
through the thoracic outlet, especially in the context of ana -
tomic variations in the supracostoclavicular space (e.g., cervical 
rib). When associated with trauma, the mechanism is typically 
upward traction of the arm overhead. Cancer can directly 
invade or compress the lower brachial plexus, as can be 
observed with a superior pulmonary sulcus (Pancoast) tumor, 
which can present with an associated Horner syndrome. Lung 
and breast cancers and lymphomas are the most common 
metastatic tumors that affect the plexus.
Thoracic outlet syndrome.  Neurogenic thoracic outlet syn -
drome (TOS) arises from compression of the brachial plexus as it 
passes through, and exits from, the thoracic outlet. This condition 
is rare, with a prevalence of 1 per 1 million, and most commonly 
affects the lower trunk of the plexus or the anterior primary rami 
of T1 or C8.
189 The plexus can be compressed at three major 
points. The first is in the interscalene triangle, as it travels between

Focal Neuropathies of the Lower Extremities
Fibular (Peroneal) Mononeuropathy
The fibular nerve arises from the L4 to S1 nerve roots and runs 
with the tibial nerve in the posterior thigh as part of the sciatic 
nerve. While still a part of the sciatic nerve at the popliteal fossa, 
it gives off a muscular branch to the short head of the biceps 
femoris, the only muscle of the thigh that is innervated by the 
fibular division of the sciatic nerve. Within the popliteal fossa, 
after dividing from the tibial nerve, it contributes a cutaneous 
branch which, along with a branch from the tibial nerve, forms 
the sural sensory nerve. Another sensory branch, the lateral cu -
taneous nerve of calf, also arises in the popliteal fossa and sup -
plies the proximal lateral aspect of the calf. The common fibular 
nerve then courses around the fibular head where it is firmly 
tethered by a fibroosseous band; it then forms its terminal 
branches, the superficial and deep fibular nerves. The superficial 
fibular nerve innervates the fibularis longus and brevis muscles 
and supplies sensory innervation to the lower two-thirds of the 
lateral leg and the dorsum of the foot, sparing the first dorsal 
web space. In 12% to 35% of the population, the superficial 
fibular nerve gives off an accessory fibular nerve, which provides 
some motor innervation to the extensor digitorum brevis 
(EDB).
173 The deep fibular nerve supplies the anterior tibialis, 
extensor hallucis longus, fibularis tertius, EDB, and sensation to 
the first web space (Fig. 42.16).

Electrodiagnosis should include a fibular motor study, with 
stimulation both above and below the fibular head to evaluate 
for focal slowing or conduction block. The fibular motor re -
sponse can be recorded over either the EDB or the anterior 
tibialis. Although the EDB is routinely used, it can be atrophic 
as a result of local trauma and may be less reliable. Focal con -

head, is the best indicator for localizing a fibular nerve injury at 
the knee and is present in 60% to 70% of lesions.
122 The pres-
ence of any response obtained at the anterior tibialis or EDB is 
associated with good recovery of grade 4 to 5 strength compared 
with absence of response.
33
Several other neurologic problems can present as foot drop. In 
addition to fibular neuropathy, the differential diagnosis also 
includes L5 radiculopathy, lumbosacral plexopathy, and sciatic 
neuropathy. The presence of back pain, prominent leg pain, a 
depressed hamstring reflex, or weakness of the hamstrings or 
plantarflexion suggest an alternate diagnosis.
If fibular nerve injury is associated with trauma, radiographs 
may demonstrate a fracture around the fibular head. MRI is  
useful for visualizing a space-occupying lesion, directly visualizing 
the fibular nerve in the popliteal fossa and identifying denervated 
muscles. Ultrasound can also visualize the nerve as it passes near 
the fibular head and can assist in identification of space-occupy -
ing lesions, such as intraneural and/or extraneural ganglion cysts 
or parameniscal cysts.
Awareness of fibular neuropathy enables prevention, especially 
in the rehabilitation setting. As many cases of fibular neuropathy 
are the result of improper positioning during prolonged hospital-
ization or surgery, care should be taken to offload the lateral as -
pect of the knee, such as padding the lateral knee with pillows and 
avoiding casts or orthoses that apply direct pressure distal to the 
fibular head. Casts and braces should be carefully evaluated and 
periodically reevaluated for pressure points at the fibular head. 
Atraumatic patients presenting with fibular neuropathy should be 
questioned regarding prolonged knee flexion activities, repetitive 
squatting, severe weight loss, and habitual leg crossing. Ankle-foot 
orthoses are often needed for more severe foot drop. Surgical 
management includes neurolysis and decompression, nerve repair, 
and nerve or tendon transfers.
Tibial and Plantar Mononeuropathy
The tibial nerve arises from the L5 to S2 nerve roots to form the 
tibial division of the sciatic nerve. While part of the sciatic nerve, 
it innervates all the hamstring muscles with the exception of the 
short head of the biceps femoris. It also partially innervates the 
adductor magnus muscle, with the obturator nerve contributing 
remaining innervation. Within the popliteal fossa, it contributes 
a sensory branch (together with a fibular nerve contribution) to 
form the sural sensory nerve, which innervates the lateral lower 
leg and foot. The tibial nerve continues on to supply the muscles 
of the posterior compartment of the lower leg. At the ankle, it 
passes just posterior to the medial malleolus through the tarsal 
tunnel, which is formed by the flexor retinaculum that spans the 
space between the medial malleolus and the calcaneus. Accompa-
nying the tibial nerve is the posterior tibial artery and the tendons 
of the posterior tibialis, flexor digitorum longus, and flexor hal -
lucis longus. The three terminal branches of the tibial nerve in the 
Fibular neuropathy is the most common nerve injury in the 
lower limb.
83 Lesions to the common fibular nerve at the fibular 
head are common because of its vulnerable superficial location 
and because it is relatively tethered by a fibrous band, which 
makes it susceptible to traction with knee and hip flexion. Causes 
of direct compression at the fibular head include prolonged pas -
sive positioning during surgery or bedrest, repeated leg crossing 
(especially with a history of recent weight loss), and improperly 
placed splints, braces, and casts. Prolonged squatting can place 
excessive traction on the nerve. Acute knee trauma, particularly 
with fibular head fracture, can also cause injury. Importantly, an 
intraneural or extraneural ganglion cyst can be a cause of direction 
compression or neural injury. These typically arise when a defect 
in the tibiofibular joint capsule enables joint fluid to accumulate 
within a cyst through a one-way valve mechanism.
158 An extra -
neural ganglion cyst from this joint can directly compress the 
fibular nerve. Alternatively, the cyst may communicate with an 
intraarticular branch of the fibular nerve, leading to an intraneu -
ral ganglion cyst, with fluid tracking proximally within the fibular 
nerve and compressing its fascicles.
The prominent clinical finding is weakness of ankle dorsi -
flexion, often with minimal pain. In severe injury, complete 
foot drop can result in a steppage gait, a compensatory mecha -
nism that allows the patient to clear the toe during the swing 
phase of gait. When weakness is more subtle, premature foot 
flat or foot slap can be seen and heard after heel strike on the 
affected side. Often the deep fibular branch is more affected 
than the superficial branch, with foot eversion less impaired 
than dorsiflexion, and with a motor deficit outweighing the 
sensory deficit. Sensation is diminished in the lower two-thirds 
of the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot. Tapping over the

Tibial Neuropathy at the Ankle
The tibial nerve can be lesioned at the medial ankle where it courses 
under the flexor retinaculum, yielding foot numbness or dysesthe-
sias in the distribution of the affected fascicles, exacerbated by 
standing and walking. When arising insidiously, it is often called 
tarsal tunnel syndrome . Compression can affect any of t he three 
terminal branches.
113 Etiologies can include ankle trauma, arthritis, 
deformity of the heel, talocalcaneal coalition,165 vascular compres-
sion, and masses such as ganglion cysts or lipomas. Physical exami-
nation can reveal sensory loss in the lateral and/or medial plantar 
distribution, often sparing the heel. A Tinel sign might be provoked 
by percussion over the flexor retinaculum at the medial malleolus.
Electrodiagnostic studies should evaluate for dysfunction of 
tibial innervation distal to the ankle. Medial and lateral plantar 
conductions can be performed as sensory, motor, or mixed nerve 
studies and can be selected based on the patient’s symptoms. Given 
the wide range of healthy nerve conduction response amplitudes 
and the high prevalence of mild neuropathy or age-related distal 
neuromuscular decline, both needle EMG of foot intrinsic muscles 
and comparison to contralateral nerve conductions where symp -
toms are unilateral are important study planning considerations.
32

Femoral Neuropathy
The femoral nerve is formed from the L2 to L4 spinal roots.  The 
nerve then travels between the psoas and iliacus muscles, innervat-
ing the iliacus just before it passes under the inguinal ligament. 
After passing under the inguinal ligament, it divides into its termi-
nal branches, which supply the four heads of the quadriceps muscle, 
the sartorius and pectineus muscles, the medial and intermediate 
cutaneous branches to the anterior thigh, and the saphenous nerve, 
which innervates the medial leg (Fig. 42.18). The femoral nerve is 
usually injured within the retroperitoneal space in the pelvis  
(e.g., compression by retroperitoneal hematoma) or under the in-
guinal ligament. Injury can be iatrogenic, as with intraabdominal 
and intrapelvic surgeries, often caused by self-retaining retractors, 
gynecologic and urologic procedures, ilioinguinal nerve blocks, and 
catheterization procedures.
36 The lithotomy position, with extreme 
hip flexion and external rotation, can result in compression of the 
femoral nerve at the inguinal ligament.
1 Thin body habitus predis-
poses to injury from retractors and the lithotomy position,183 while 
obese patients are at greater risk of femoral nerve injury in high-
energy pelvic trauma.
93
Femoral neuropathy usually presents with unilateral thigh 
weakness and numbness of the anterior thigh and medial leg. A 
sensation of instability around the knee is usually noted, with 
particular difficulty with stairs and inclines. The patellar reflex  
can be depressed or absent. If the nerve is compressed within the 
pelvis, hip flexion strength can also be affected. Adduction and 
abduction of the thigh should be unaffected, as should knee

Electrodiagnostic studies can include a femoral motor conduc-
tion study, stimulating at the groin. A CMAP of at least 50% of 
the opposite side favors good prognosis for improvement within 
1 year, whereas fewer than half of those with a CMAP of less than 
50% will improve.
89 Needle examination may help in localizing 
the site of the lesion to the inguinal ligament or intrapelvic based 
on involvement of the iliopsoas muscle. Study of the hip adductor 
muscles and paraspinal muscles will help exclude high lumbar 
radiculopathy or plexopathy. MRI and CT imaging are indicated 
if there is suspicion for a mass lesion.
Many causes of femoral neuropathy are iatrogenic and may 
be avoidable. In operative procedures, extremes of flexion, exter -
nal rotation, and abduction of the hip should be avoided to 
prevent compression as the nerve passes under the inguinal liga -
ment. Self-retaining retractors are also a potential source of in -
jury during surgery, and care should be taken with placement to 
avoid compressing the nerve as it passes the psoas muscle.
16 
When a femoral neuropathy is suspected, imaging studies can 
assess for compression caused by hematoma, masses, or pseu -
doaneurysm. If a correctable cause is found, immediate surgical 
intervention might be indicated. Early physical therapy for 
strengthening and ROM should be initiated. T raining to acti -
vate the gluteal muscles and/or plantar flexors in stance phase 
can help to augment weak knee extension. With mild to moder -
ate knee extension weakness, an ankle-foot orthosis with dorsi -
flexion stop can create an extension moment at the knee to 
compensate. If the weakness is severe, a knee-ankle-foot orthosis 
may be indicated; however, these are cumbersome and often 
poorly tolerated. Therefore a walker to keep the ground reaction 
force anterior to the knee axis of rotation during stance is often 
more functional. Serial clinical and electrodiagnostic examina -
tions should be performed. If there is no improvement after 3 to 
6 months, either clinically or electrodiagnostically, then surgical 
intervention might be indicated.
36 Because the quadriceps is 
such a large muscle, it is important to extensively sample the 
muscle for signs of reinnervation, as early recovery in the more 
proximal portions may be missed if only sampled in the more 
typically studied middistal portions.
Lateral Femoral Cutaneous Neuropathy
Mononeuropathy of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, also 
known as meralgia paresthetica, is a common and benign cause of 
anterolateral thigh numbness. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve 
is a purely sensory nerve arising from the L2 and L3 nerve roots. 
There are several anatomic variations as the nerve exits the pelvis, 
most commonly in close relationship with the anterior superior 
iliac spine (ASIS) and the inguinal ligament, making the nerve 
vulnerable to compression in this area.
7 The nerve then divides 
into anterior and posterior divisions and supplies the sensory ter -
ritory of the anterolateral thigh ( Fig. 42.19). 30 Meralgia pares-
thetica is typically idiopathic and is associated with obesity, 
pregnancy, and diabetes.
74 The nerve may be externally com -
pressed by heavy tool belts, habitually leaning against a table, or 
wearing tight waist bands. Rarely, injury can occur during surgery, 
particularly during hernia repair, renal transplantation, harvesting 
of iliac bone graft, hip surgery, and aortic valve and coronary by -
pass surgery.
152 A tumor involving the iliac crest can also compress 
the nerve at the ASIS, or more proximally it can be affected by an 
abdominal aneurysm or other pelvic mass.
Patients with lateral femoral cutaneous nerve dysfunction present 
with numbness and pain in a generally well-circumscribed distribu-
tion over the anterolateral thigh. Symptoms can be exacerbated by 
walking or extension of the hip and by wearing tight clothing or 
undergarments. No weakness is seen. On physical examination, in 
addition to numbness or paresthesia in the territory of the lateral 
femoral cutaneous nerve, a Tinel sign may be elicited when per-
cussing just medial and inferior to the ASIS. Manual motor testing 
and muscle stretch reflexes are not affected. Meralgia paresthetica 
must be differentiated from femoral neuropathy, lumbar plexopathy, 
and high lumbar radiculopathy. This can often be done clinically, 
based on the distribution of sensory symptoms. Femoral and lumbar 
radiculopathies, unlike meralgia paresthetica, can also cause weakness 
of thigh muscles and a depressed patellar reflex.
Nerve conduction studies of the lateral femoral cutaneous 
nerve may reveal asymmetry when comparing the symptomatic 
with the unaffected side. However, this is a technically difficult 
sensory response to obtain even in healthy, nonobese individu -
als.
1,90 Needle EMG is normal in those with meralgia paresthetica. 
Although not routinely performed, ultrasound can improve the 
ability to obtain a nerve conduction response by visualizing the 
nerve.
14 Anesthetic nerve block of the lateral femoral cutaneous 
nerve may help confirm the diagnosis, although the anatomic 
variability of the nerve may lead to a negative block.
Meralgia paresthetica is a benign and self-limited condition. It 
often improves with conservative treatment over a few months. 
Eliminating exacerbating factors, such as tight clothing or tool  
belts, and weight loss will often resolve the symptoms. When painful

Sciatic Mononeuropathy
The sciatic nerve is made up of fibers from the L4 to S2 nerve 
roots, forming the tibial and fibular divisions of the sciatic 
nerve. These divisions run as distinctly separate nerves that do 
not interchange fascicles. The fibular division is much more 
susceptible to injury than the tibial division, for two main rea -
sons. First, the fibular nerve is tethered at both the sciatic notch 
and the fibular head, making it more vulnerable to traction than 
the tibial nerve, which is not similarly restricted distally. Second, 
the fascicular pattern of the fibular nerve contains less support -
ing tissues and has larger and fewer fascicles than the tibial 
nerve, which is more invested with elastic epineural tissue and is 
therefore more tolerant of external pressure.
161 This propensity 
for greater injury to the fibular division of the sciatic nerve can 
make it challenging to differentiate a sciatic lesion from a com-
mon fibular lesion. The tibial innervated hamstring and plantar 
flexor muscles are powerful, making subtle weakness less obvi -
ous than with muscles innervated by the fibular nerve. Causes of 
sciatic nerve injury include hip fracture, posterior hip disloca -
tion, and hip surgery, particularly total hip arthroplasty. Poorly 
placed intramuscular gluteal injections, knife or gunshot 
wounds, compression resulting from positioning during hospi-
talization in patients with thin body habitus, and the lithotomy 
position
183 can also injure the nerve. Space-occupying lesions 
such as tumors, hematoma, aneurysm, and heterotopic ossifica -
tion can also compress the nerve.
As discussed previously with fibular neuropathy, electrodiagnos-
tic studies are designed to differentiate between sciatic neuropathy, 
common fibular or tibial mononeuropathy, L5 or S1 radiculopathy, 
and lumbosacral plexopathy. In severe sciatic neuropathy, all the 
fibular and tibial motor conduction responses are absent or reduced, 
as are sensory responses of the sural and superficial fibular nerves. In 
mild cases, side-to-side asymmetry of sensory and motor nerve con-
duction amplitudes should be evaluated. Needle examination of 
both fibular and tibial innervated muscles show denervation, usually 
with fibular muscles more affected. Occasionally, the fibular division 
can be exclusively injured in a sciatic lesion, in which case sampling 
of the short head of the biceps femoris is useful to differentiate it 
from a more distal common fibular lesion.
84 Gluteal and paraspinal 
muscles will be spared in sciatic lesions. MRI can demonstrate com-
pressive lesions and, along with a CT scan, characterize traumatic 
injury. The sciatic nerve itself can be well visualized with MRI, show-
ing focal enlargement and abnormal signal intensity. Ultrasound is 
not useful in evaluating the nerve within the pelvis; however, it may 
be useful in evaluating the nerve below the piriformis muscle.
101
T reatment of sciatic nerve injury is largely supportive, with 
more specific management directed at etiology of the injury. If a 
compressive lesion is identified, such as tumor or hematoma, de -
compression may be indicated. Nerve grafting outcomes are 
guarded because of the size and length of the sciatic nerve; key 
functional muscles are often at great distance from the nerve  
lesion, precluding functional reinnervation.

Lumbosacral Plexopathies
The lumbosacral plexus can be divided into the lumbar and sacral 
plexuses. The lumbar portion is formed within the psoas muscle 
from the L1 to L3 nerve roots with contributions from T12 and 
L4. The lumbar plexus provides direct branches to the psoas 
muscle. The terminal branches are the ilioinguinal, iliohypogas-
tric, genitofemoral, femoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, and obtu -
rator nerves. The sacral plexus forms within the pelvis anterior to 
the piriformis muscle. T erminal branches include the nerves to 
the superior/inferior gemelli, obturator internus, piriformis, supe-
rior and inferior gluteal nerves, posterior femoral cutaneous 
nerve, and sciatic nerve (Fig. 42.20).
Lumbar Plexus
Retroperitoneal hemorrhage preferentially affects the lumbar por-
tion of the plexus, usually in association with anticoagulation, 
blood dyscrasias (e.g., hemophilia), or leaking aortic aneurysm. 
This lesion affects the femoral innervation disproportionately, rela-
tive to obturator nerve innervation. Vascular aneurysms can also 
lesion the lumbar plexus directly, even without overt bleeding.
The lumbar and lumbosacral plexuses can also be affected an 
inflammatory or vasculitic disease processes. In particular, a dia -
betic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy (DLRPN; formerly 
known as diabetic amyotrophy) can involve the lumbar plexus. 
The pathophysiology is felt to reflect microscopic vasculitis, with 
ischemia leading to axonal nerve injury. A typical presentation is 
one of asymmetric pain in an upper lumbar distribution, followed 
by weakness and atrophy, often disproportionate to the sensory 
loss. It can be associated with weight loss and autonomic derange-
ments.
Lumbosacral Trunk
The lumbosacral trunk carries the L4 and L5 contributions to the 
sacral plexus, coursing close to the sacrum as it traverses the pelvic 
outlet. The structure can be injured during labor and delivery, 
with risk factors including cephalopelvic disproportion, abnormal 
fetal presentation, and use of forceps during delivery. The lesion 
presents as a foot drop, with a broader span of weakness, includ -
ing knee flexors, hip abductors, and internal rotators. Unlike an 
L4 radiculopathy, muscles innervated through t he lumbar plexus 
(quadriceps, adductors) are spared with this lesion localization, 
while L4 contribution to the sciatic nerve (e.g., innervation to 
anterior tibialis) is affected.
In the pregnancy or delivery context, the lesion is typically 
caused by compression or stretch. There is often a two-stage re-
covery, with recovery of the demyelinating component within 
days to weeks, and a slower recovery for any axonal loss (months 
to years).

Sacral Plexus
The sacral plexus is well protected within the bony pelvis. When 
lesioned traumatically, the mechanism is typically one of high 
force, often with concomitant pelvic, hip, or sacroiliac joint 
trauma. Concurrent root avulsion can occur, usually in associa -
tion with separation of the sacroiliac joint. Incomplete lesions are 
often initially overlooked because of pain and immobility from 
the orthopedic injury or from altered mental status from concur -
rent brain injury or intubation. It is not uncommon to detect 
these injuries during consultation in the trauma unit or after the 
patient has begun the rehabilitation course; therefore it is impor -
tant to have a high index of suspicion for plexus and other periph-
eral nerve injury in the setting of trauma.
The sacral plexus can also be injured by compressive lesions. 
T umor can injure the plexus through compression by mass effect 
from primary disease or nodal metastases or by direct infiltration 
of the neural structure as by tumor cells. Commonly involved 
malignancies include those of the prostate, bladder, uterus, cervix, 
colon, and rectum. Lymphoma and chordoma can also be impli -
cated. Plexopathy caused by malignancy usually presents with 
pain as the predominant symptom, with weakness and sensory 
loss occurring later.
EDX is helpful in confirming a plexopathy and documenting 
the severity and extent of the lesion. It may also be useful in dif -
ferentiating between plexopathy, radiculopathy, and individual 
mononeuropathy. MRI and CT imaging are important to evalu -
ate for injuries in the setting of trauma or compressive lesions in 
nontraumatic cases.
Regional Nerve Disorders
Neuralgic Amyotrophy
Neuralgic amyotrophy (also known as Parsonage-T urner syn -
drome and acute brachial neuritis) classically presents with abrupt 
onset of severe pain often affecting the scapula and upper arm or 
neck. Within days to about 2 weeks, this pain is followed by 
weakness of the muscles of the shoulder girdle and upper limb, 
and paresthesias in a patchy, nondermatomal distribution. An 
antecedent immunologic event, such as immunization, infection, 
surgery, or pregnancy in the days or weeks prior, is commonly 
identified. Mechanical factors may also play a role, with focal 
strain causing disturbances in the perineural blood barrier. This, 
in the context of immune system activation, leads to autoimmune 
damage to nerve segments.72
Neuralgic amyotrophy has a predilection for certain neural 
structures, likely driven by mechanical and anatomic factors; 
these include the upper brachial plexus and the suprascapular, 
long thoracic, axillary, radial, posterior interosseous, anterior in -
terosseous, and musculocutaneous nerves. 171 Though typically 
unilateral, some involvement of the contralateral side is seen in up 
to one-third of cases. Recent literature estimates the incidence of 
classic neuralgic amyotrophy to be 1 per 1000 persons per year in 
the general population. 175 The average age of onset is 40 years, 
although an autosomal dominant form, or hereditary neuralgic 
amyotrophy, has an average age of onset around 20 years. 20 Neu-
ralgic amyotrophy should be especially considered when patients 
report severe pain that resolves and is followed by weakness, when 
sensory abnormalities are vague and patchy, and when the degree 
of weakness is significant. EDX can assist with localization and 
exclude differential diagnoses. Sensory nerve conduction studies 
are often normal, although they can show axonal loss in cases 
where patients have distinct sensory fascicular involvement. 148,149 
Needle EMG may demonstrate denervation potentials in the 
distribution of nerves affected. Ultrasound can aid in the diagno-
sis when it reveals focal nerve or fascicular swelling, nerve con -
striction, and fascicular entwinement, although not all cases will 
show abnormality. These abnormalities may represent a contin -
uum of disease, with nerve swelling occurring first, followed by 
local adhesion formation and constriction, and finally fascicular 
torsion.24
Initial treatment of neuralgic amyotrophy focuses on pain 
management with analgesics. A role for corticosteroids, adminis -
tered early, in decreasing the immune-mediated damage has been 
suggested.176,179 Shoulder ROM should be maintained, and or -
thoses should be used as appropriate to assist function. Prognosis 
is generally good; however, recovery might occur over several 
years, with 85% recovering over 3 years.171 Residual weakness can 
lead to musculoskeletal disorders, as commonly occurs when 
scapular stabilizer weakness predisposes to rotator cuff pain.  
Accordingly, exercises to build endurance of scapular muscles and 
activity modification that includes frequent rest breaks in a supine 
position can be helpful. 178 A potential role for surgical manage -
ment, especially where recovery is poor and imaging reveals 
hourglass-like constrictions or fascicular torsion, is emerging and 
warrants further study.88
Diabetic and Nondiabetic Lumbosacral 
Radiculoplexus Neuropathy
DLRPN is also known by the terms diabetic amyotrophy  and 
Bruns-Garland syndrome.  It classically presents acutely or sub -
acutely in older patients with type 2 diabetes with severe pain that 
is unilateral or bilateral, and it is often accompanied by weight 
loss. The pain tends to attenuate over weeks to months, accompa-
nied by marked atrophy of the thigh muscles; it affects particu -
larly the quadriceps, adductors, and iliopsoas, with relative  
sparing of gluteal and hamstring muscles. Sensory abnormalities 
are usually seen in the femoral and occasionally saphenous distri -
bution. A proximal neuropathy affecting the thoracic dermatomes 
can less commonly occur, presenting with severe abdominal or 
chest pain, associated with weight loss, which may prompt con -
cern for malignancy. The mechanism of injury is thought to be an 
immune-mediated microvasculitis affecting the nerve roots and 
plexus. It is usually seen in diabetic patients with preexisting distal 
neuropathy. A variant can present with acute and debilitating pain 
in a younger patient with early, mild diabetes (or previously undi-
agnosed diabetes), with no evidence of distal neuropathy. The 
presentation can also arise absent diabetes mellitus, in which case 
it is termed lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy.
Electrodiagnostic studies show axonal, sensorimotor lesions of 
the nerve supply to affected muscles, often with paraspinal muscle 
abnormalities, reflective of an underlying radiculoplexus neuropa-
thy in an upper lumbar distribution. Distal nerve conduction 
studies often show evidence of a concomitant distal neuropathy 
even in patients without clinical complaints of distal neuropathic 
symptoms.
Management of DLRPN consists of tight glycemic control 
and aggressive management of neuropathic pain. Corticoste -
roids, plasmapheresis, and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 
have not shown efficacy. 21 Even though the condition is 
extremely debilitating, the prognosis for improvement is good, 
with recovery progressing for up to 3 years, although it may be 
incomplete.25

Radiation-Induced Nerve Injury
Radiation-induced nerve injury is a late effect of treatment, pre -
senting 0.5 to 20 years after treatment, 75 and is dose related.  
Radiation injury is caused by both direct toxic effects on the axon 
and damage to the vasa nervorum, causing ischemia. Radiation-
induced nerve injury tends to present with numbness, sensory 
changes, and weakness, without much pain and with gradual 
progression over many months or years. Electrodiagnostic find -
ings include denervation and myokymic discharges, although 
these findings are not specific to this disorder.
Management of radiation-induced nerve injury is primarily 
supportive, with therapy to maintain ROM and to prevent con-
tractures, bracing, adaptive measures, management of lymph -
edema, and prevention of skin breakdown. Occasionally, nerve 
transfer or reconstruction has been utilized in select patients with 
radiation-induced nerve injury to improve function.
172
Other Pain Syndromes
Several syndromes of limb pain may represent peripheral nerve 
entrapment syndromes, which can sometimes be confirmed struc-
turally (via imaging or surgical exploration) or physiologically (via 
EDX). Where diagnostic testing is unrevealing, diagnosis of these 
entities as mononeuropathies is controversial. Clinical presenta -
tions within a given category likely comprise a heterogeneous array 
of pathologies, ranging from pain syndromes unrelated to periph-
eral nerves, other musculoskeletal or neurologic diagnoses, nerve 
irritation without frank damage, to physiologically confirmable 
mononeuropathies with focal neurologic deficits. The variability  
of diagnostic criteria across studies limits systematic study of  
these disorders. Accordingly, where these pain disorders are sus -
pected, treatment should be tailored based on the presenting 
symptoms and the presence or absence of objective diagnostic 
findings. Table 42.6 lists common pain syndromes that may, in 
some cases, represent underlying peripheral nerve entrapments.
Lower Abdominal Neuropathies
Pain affecting the lower abdomen and genital region can be caused 
by injury to the ilioinguinal, iliohypogastric, and genitofemoral 
nerves. These can be diagnostically challenging, and patients often 
present after an unfruitful gastroenterologic, urologic, or gyneco -
logic workup. Localization is challenging because these nerves  
have overlapping and variable innervation patterns ( Table 42.7). 
Typically, these neuropathies will present with pain or abnormal 
sensation in their cutaneous distributions. Weakness of the ante -
rior abdominal muscles can contribute to hernia formation. A Ti-
nel sign can sometimes be elicited near the ASIS. The cremasteric 
reflex may be lost. Extension of the spine puts tension on these 
nerves, leading patients to adopt a forward flexed posture.
Frequent areas of entrapment or injury include the psoas muscle, 
the transverse abdominus muscle, and the inguinal ligament and 
canal. These injuries are frequently iatrogenic and associated with 
Pfannenstiel incisions, the placement of lateral trocars in inguinal 
hernia repairs, and surgeries with low transverse incisions such as 
abdominoplasty and cesarean section. The nerves may be com-
pressed by space-occupying lesions of the psoas muscle, entrapment
