Generalized Peripheral Neuropathies
Generalized peripheral polyneuropathies are characterized by 
their pattern of involvement and by their underlying patho -
physiology. The majority of insidious and gradually progressive 
peripheral polyneuropathies involve both sensory and motor 
fascicles in a length-dependent manner, with distal segments 
most affected. The regional pattern and spread are aptly described 
as a “stocking” and, eventually, “stocking-glove” distribution. 
The primary pathophysiology is typically axonal rather than 
demyelinating. An understanding of these typical features (Fig. 
42.21) can aid in recognizing this pattern and can serve as a 
reference point for understanding the atypical patterns of 
neuropathy.
Typical Neuropathy: Clinical Presentation
Patients typically describe a gradual onset and distal to proximal 
progression of numbness, tingling, or sensory loss over months or 
years. Onset, beginning in the toes or forefeet, is gradual enough 
that there is usually not a hallmark date or event. Positive symp-
toms include paresthesias and dysesthesias. Patients may experi -
ence negative symptoms (diminished ability to feel), describing 
their feet as feeling like “wood” or that the plantar surface of their 
feet feels indistinct when walking. Pain, if present, may be a dull, 
cramping ache. Functionally, patients may describe difficulty 
maintaining balance with higher level tasks that rely on proprio -
ceptive input, such as walking on uneven terrain or at night in the 
dark. Impaired balance (and even falls) can be the initial present-
ing complaint and has significant and sometimes dangerous func-
tional consequences. Any patient who presents with repeated falls

Causes
Common causes of typical axonal neuropathy include diabetes 
mellitus, alcohol toxicity, hypothyroidism renal failure, and 
metabolic syndrome. Impaired glucose tolerance and, specifi-
cally, the severity and duration of hyperglycemia are major risk 
factors.
156,52 Other risk factors include hypertension, elevated 
triglycerides, tobacco use, obesity, microvascular disease, and 
cardiovascular disease.
166 Where impaired glucose tolerance is 
implicated, counseling on the importance and role of lifestyle 
modifications is important. Strict glycemic control has been 
shown to reduce the prevalence of polyneuropathy by almost 
70% and autonomic dysfunction by over 50%.
35 It has also been 
shown to reverse polyneuropathy.37,121
As neuropathy progresses, more body regions become involved 
in a length-dependent pattern. When lower limb symptoms have 
progressed as far proximally as the knees, distal finger involvement 
may begin. In advanced stages, the intercostal and abdominal 
nerves can be affected, leading to numbness over the more distal 
cutaneous regions, near the sternum and central abdomen.
Physical Examination
Exam for suspected neuropathy should incorporate components 
of the sensory and motor systems and an assessment for function 
and balance.
Sensory Examination
On physical exam, assessment of vibratory sensation can quantify 
sensory function. The use of a tuning fork for detection of vibra -
tory loss can detect neuropathy even when monofilament testing 
is normal.
117 The Romberg test can also detect impaired balance 
related to decreased sensory function. Skin lesions or ulceration 
may be seen in an insensate foot.
Motor Examination
In early typical neuropathy, the motor examination is normal. 
Longstanding neuropathy may evidence atrophy of the foot—
and eventually hand—intrinsic muscles. Chronic atrophy and 
weakness lead to foot deformities, such as claw toes, hammertoes, 
and even deformity and collapse of the midfoot architecture. 
Functionally, motor deficits are often characterized by easy 
fatigability. T esting the muscle multiple consecutive times can 
improve detection of subtle weakness. Occasionally, fasciculations 
can be seen.
Reflexes
Reflexes are typically impaired in a length-dependent fashion, 
with the Achilles reflex first involved.
Autonomic Assessment
Autonomic dysfunction can result in decreased sweating and dry, 
cold feet. Small fiber neuropathy can result in increased blood 
flow and “hot foot.”
Assessment of Gait and Balance
Unipedal stance time is a sensitive test of balance impairment. 
Decreased unipedal stance time is associated with a diffuse pe -
ripheral neuropathy in young and middle-aged males
71 and is 
strongly correlated with frontal plane sensory and motor function

Challenges in the Elderly Population
Distinguishing the normal distal decline in neuromuscular 
function associated with aging from that resulting from disease 
can be difficult. Physical findings predictive of electrodiagnostic 
confirmation of peripheral neuropathy in this population  
are loss of the Achilles reflex, inability to perceive at least 8 of 
10 movements of 0.5 to 1 cm, or being unable to detect a vi -
brating 128-Hz tuning fork for at least 8 seconds at the great 
toe (Box 42.1 ).131
Electrophysiologic Assessment
Electrophysiologic examination should be designed to character -
ize the neuropathy: whether the typical axonal pattern or a less 
typical subtype. Confirmation and characterization of an atypical 
subtype is a critical step toward accurate diagnosis and appropri -
ate management.
The electrophysiologic examination is tailored to the patient 
and designed to assess the following features.
Fiber Type Involvement: Sensory, Motor, or Both?
Sensory and motor nerve fascicles should be studied distally to 
assess this parameter. In its earliest phase, a typical axonal poly -
neuropathy may evidence sensory abnormalities alone (subtly 
decreased SNAP amplitudes). However, in more advanced 
neuropathy, these should be accompanied by motor abnormali -
ties, possibly reinnervation changes observed in distal muscles 
on EMG (MUAPs with increased amplitude, duration and/or 
polyphasia). A pattern of very low amplitude or absent SNAPs 
combined with robustly normal CMAPs and no EMG abnor -
malities raises questions of a sensory neuropathy or neuronopa -
thy. These pathophysiologic patterns are important to identify 
because they may reflect a treatable disorder (such as a nutri -
tional neuropathy), or they may herald a broader disease pro -
cess important to identify (such as Sjögren syndrome or a 
paraneoplastic phenomenon). Conversely, the finding of mo -
tor-predominant or motor-only abnormalities on EDX study is 
also notable and may indicate a hereditary disorder or atypical 
motor predominant process.
Notably, standard EDX testing does not assess small fibers. 
Pure small fiber neuropathy is likely rare; many patients with 
small fiber predominant symptoms have been shown to also 
have large fiber involvement. However, a pure small fiber neu -
ropathy, manifesting primarily with burning pain or changes in 
temperature perception, cannot be adequately assessed by stan -
dard EDX testing.
Regional Distribution: Is There Length Dependence or a 
Different Pattern?
Length dependence can be assessed electrophysiologically, com -
paring distal lower limbs (longest nerves) with distal upper limbs, 
and, sometimes, comparing relatively more proximal responses 
with distal ones (e.g., a fibular motor study recording anterior 
tibialis assesses a more proximal segment than the motor study 
recording EDB). Needle EMG should include a selection of 
proximal and distal muscles to assess for a length-dependent gra -
dient of abnormality. Peripheral polyneuropathies in their mildest 
phases may manifest abnormalities only in distal muscles, such as 
foot intrinsics.
Notably, muscles affected very chronically by advanced neu -
ropathy may not show fibrillation potentials. In advanced neu -
ropathy, the most distal muscles may reveal decreased insertional 
activity (reflective of muscle fiber atrophy) in lieu of fibrillation 
potentials. Fibrillation potentials and reinnervation changes may 
be observed only in more proximal muscles that still have viable 
fibers (e.g., the leg segment).
Findings of nonlength dependence, including prominent 
proximal muscle involvement early in disease course, or marked 
asymmetry with any given level of length dependence, should 
raise question of a different pathophysiologic pattern (e.g., nerve 
by nerve, multifocal, or myotomal).
Axonal or Demyelinating?
Typical peripheral polyneuropathy is predominantly axonal, man-
ifesting low-amplitude SNAPs and CMAPs. Latencies may be 
moderately prolonged, and conduction velocities may be moder -
ately slowed, commensurate with loss of the fastest fibers. Slowing 
of CV beyond about 75% of the lower limit of reference values, 
or prolongation of distal latency beyond about 130% of the upper 
limit of reference values should raise the question of demyelinat -
ing pathophysiology, especially where amplitudes remain moder -
ately well preserved. Demyelination can also be assessed using late 
responses (e.g., F-waves), which offer increased sensitivity by as -
sessing a longer conduction distance and by inclusion of proximal 
segments (such as nerve roots, which can be involved early in in -
flammatory demyelinating neuropathies).
In contrast to axonal neuropathies, acquired demyelinating 
disorders, in addition to showing slowing of CV and prolongation 
of distal latencies, often show conduction block, abnormal tem -
poral dispersion, and distal CMAP dispersion. Abnormal tempo -
ral dispersion and increased distal CMAP dispersion both reflect 
desynchrony of conduction within a nerve segment.
On the other hand, hereditary demyelinating neuropathies 
typically show uniform slowing of conduction velocities, without 
significantly increased temporal dispersion or conduction block. 
In these disorders, the myelin conducts diffusely slowly, but with-
out the conduction blocks or desynchrony observed in patchy, 
acquired demyelination.
Finally, it is notable that some conduction block reflects 
nodo-paranodopathy rather than segmental demyelination of the 
internodes. An understanding of this pathophysiology has re -
cently emerged, and it is tied to several atypical neuropathies that 
involve immune-mediated destruction at the nodal or paranodal 
regions of nerves. It is possible for a nodo-paranodopathy to 
manifest with conduction block, absent the temporal dispersion, 
distal CMAP dispersion, and marked conduction slowing more

GBS presents with progressive onset of limb weakness, both 
proximally and distally, that is typically symmetric, with strength 
nadir in 2 to 4 weeks. Reflexes are lost early in the disease, al -
though reflexes can be retained or even brisk with axonal forms. 
Sensory loss is variable. The cranial nerves can be affected, with 
facial palsy and bulbar weakness. Muscles of respiration are fre -
quently affected, leading to decline of vital capacity and requiring  
ventilatory support in 25% of cases. Autonomic dysfunction can 
result in tachycardia, hypertension, and cardiac arrhythmias. Pain 
can be significant and can precede the onset of clinical weakness. 
In the acute stages, the pain is described as deep and aching,  
affecting the back, buttocks, and posterior thighs, caused by no -
ciceptive pain from inflammation. Later, neuropathic pain can be 
described, with degeneration and regeneration of sensory nerves. 
Fatigue is common and persists even after good neurologic recov-
ery. Amantadine and other pharmacologic agents have not helped, 
but bicycle exercise training has been shown to reduce fatigue and 
improve functional outcome and quality of life.
51
T wo-thirds of patients with GBS have an antecedent infection 
within 3 weeks of onset of weakness. The most commonly identi-
fied infectious agent is Campylobacter jejuni. Other causative 
agents include cytomegalovirus (CMV), Epstein-Barr virus,  
Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and Haemophilus influenzae.61 Although 
studies of the swine flu vaccine during the 1976 epidemic demon-
strated an increased risk of one excess case of GBS for every 
100,000 people vaccinated,
144 there is little evidence to link GBS 
with other vaccines, including newer influenza vaccines. 59 The 
strong association with antecedent infection suggests that at least 
in some cases there is an immune response to these infectious 
agents that triggers damage to peripheral nerves. The preceding 
infectious agent is also associated with the subtype of GBS that 
develops. Nerves are initially affected at the root level, followed by 
distal segments and the intervening areas later in the course of the 
disease.
9
Required clinical features for the diagnosis of GBS are progres-
sive weakness in both arms and legs, and areflexia. Supportive 
features include progression of weakness over days to 4 weeks, 
symmetry of symptoms, mild sensory symptoms, cranial nerve 
involvement, autonomic involvement, pain (which can be promi-
nent), high protein concentration in the cerebrospinal fluid 
(CSF), and typical electrodiagnostic findings. A number of fea -
tures raise doubts about the diagnosis of GBS (Box 42.2).
Lumbar puncture is routinely performed in the workup of 
patients presenting with rapidly progressive weakness. The charac-
teristic finding in GBS is an elevated CSF protein with normal 
white blood cell count (,5–10 3 10
6 cells/L), referred to as  
albuminocytologic dissociation. EDX is the most useful confirma -
tory test for GBS and differentiates between the more common 
demyelinating form (AIDP) and the axonal forms (AMAN, AM -
SAN). Nerve conduction studies can be normal in early AIDP , 
and electrodiagnostic findings usually lag behind clinical severity. 
As the nerve roots are typically affected first, prolongation of the  
F-wave latencies is the earliest abnormality seen, as they measure 
conduction in the proximal nerve segments.
2 Nerve conduction 
studies demonstrate a demyelinating pattern, with motor involve-
ment occurring earlier and more extensively than sensory abnor-
malities. Sensory changes tend to be patchier in distribution, and 
median and ulnar sensory responses tend to be affected more than 
the sural response. T emporal dispersion and partial conduction 
block can be seen between proximal and distal stimulation sites. 
Needle examination shows decreased firing of motor units on 
maximal contraction. An axonal variant of GBS is suggested if 
there is significant reduction in CMAP amplitudes and early and 
significant findings of abnormal insertional activity on needle 
examination. CMAP amplitudes that fall below 20% of the lower 
limit of normal predict poor functional outcome.
106 Nerve ultra-
sound may demonstrate diffuse or segmental nerve enlargement 
and may be useful in the diagnosis of atypical presentations.
151
Management of GBS requires a multidisciplinary approach 
consisting of supportive care, disease-modifying treatment, and 
rehabilitation. About one-third of those affected require intensive 
care unit admission.
77 Mortality for GBS is 10%, with infections, 
pulmonary embolus, and cardiac arrhythmia the most common 
causes of death. Deep venous thrombosis prophylaxis should be 
initiated, particularly in nonambulatory patients. Vital capacity 
should be sequentially monitored every 2 to 4 hours initially. 
Mechanical ventilation should be initiated if vital capacity falls 
below 20 mL/kg.

Gastrointestinal involvement is the most common autonomic 
abnormality with ileus occurring in 15% of those with GBS. 5 In 
some patients, pain can be difficult to treat, requiring opioids, 
gabapentin, and other agents for neuropathic pain. Prevention of 
pressure ulcers with proper positioning and turning, as well as 
ROM and splinting to prevent contractures, should be instituted. 
Swallowing function should be monitored, particularly in those 
with signs of facial or bulbar involvement.
Both plasma exchange (PE) and IVIG have been proven to be 
of benefit in altering the course of GBS. 56 IVIG has been shown 
to be as effective as PE 45 and is usually the preferred treatment 
because of greater convenience and availability, and fewer com -
plications. Combining PE and IVIG has not been shown to be 
superior to monotherapy.123 Forty percent to 50% of patients do 
not improve with either treatment; however, there is no evidence 
of benefit to repeating treatment or changing to an alternate 
treatment.181 Eight percent to 16% of patients develop neuro -
logic deterioration after initial improvement after treatment. 87 
It  is common practice to administer a second course of IVIG  
in these patients. Oral or intravenous steroids have not been 
shown to be effective in altering the course or functional 
outcome of GBS. 69
Rehabilitation of patients begins in the acute phase with pre -
vention of contractures with proper ROM, positioning, and 
splinting. Mobilization as soon as the patient’s medical condition 
permits is essential in minimizing the deleterious effects of bed -
rest. Intensive pulmonary hygiene reduces the risk of atelectasis 
and pneumonia. Formal inpatient rehabilitation is initiated when 
immunotherapy has been completed, and the neurologic deterio-
ration has reached its nadir. Autonomic and pulmonary status 
should be stabilized, and the patient should either be weaned 
from the ventilator or transferred to a facility that has ventilator 
capabilities. Orthostatic hypotension is a common problem, and 
use of a tilt table may be necessary before initiating standing and 
gait training. Lower extremity orthoses are often needed when 
initiating ambulation. Functional and weight-training exercises 
are used for upper extremity strengthening, and assistive devices 
are employed for self-care activities. Exercise in GBS patients 
should be nonfatiguing because patients can be prone to declines 
in strength if exercise is advanced too rapidly (particularly in 
muscles with less than antigravity strength).
Prognosis of GBS is generally good, especially given the often-
marked initial degree of weakness. T wenty percent of patients will 
have persistent disability, remaining nonambulatory or requiring 
an assistive device to walk at 6 months, and many have residual 
fatigue and pain.70 Poor outcome is associated with advanced age, 
male sex, axonal involvement, antecedent diarrhea, and CMV 
infection.126 Relapse is rare at a rate of 3% to 5%. 61
Chronic Inflammatory Demyelinating 
Polyradiculoneuropathy
CIDP is an immune-mediated disorder of the peripheral nervous 
system. Classically, CIDP is a symmetric neuropathy. Unlike 
typical polyneuropathies, which are length dependent, CIDP 
presents with both proximal and distal involvement. Variants  
include Lewis-Sumner syndrome, which has a multifocal presen -
tation variably affecting motor and sensory nerve, and purely 
sensory forms, as well as forms associated with immunoglobulin 
G (IgG) and IgA paraproteins, and central nervous system (CNS) 
demyelination. A number of conditions also are important to  
differentiate from CIDP variants, especially because they respond 
differently to treatment. These include multifocal motor neuropa-
thy and IgM-related neuropathies, such as POEMS ( polyneu-
ropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M protein, and skin 
changes).
CIDP typically progresses over at least 2 months, which dif -
ferentiates it from GBS. Motor symptoms generally predominate, 
and both proximal and distal muscles are affected. Because CIDP 
is a primarily demyelinating disorder, muscle atrophy is minimal. 
Muscle stretch reflexes are reduced or absent. Cranial involvement 
occurs in 10% to 20% of cases, and a small number of patients 
have painful dysesthesias. Sensory involvement preferentially  
affects large fiber nerves, consequently preferentially affecting vi -
bration and proprioception. Sensory symptoms progress distally 
to proximally, although hand involvement is often perceived as 
early as that in the feet. Respiratory and cranial nerve involvement 
is rare. CIDP typically shows a pattern of recurrence and remit -
tance (polyphasic) in younger patients. 112 Older adults are more 
likely to have a progressive form of CIDP (monophasic). A small 
number (20%) may have a subacute onset (4 to 8 weeks) or an 
acute GBS-like symptom onset before it eventually evolves into a 
relapsing form. Unlike AIDP , an antecedent infection is typically 
not identified.40
Electrodiagnostic studies are critical for confirmation of CIDP . 
Nerve conduction studies show a pattern of primary demyelin -
ation affecting multiple segments. Findings consistent with demy-
elination include conduction block, conduction slowing that is 
greater than can be explained by axon loss, prolonged F-wave and 
H-reflex latencies, and temporal dispersion of CMAP between 
proximal and distal stimulation points. A nonuniform pattern is 
also suggestive of CIDP . Upper limb sensory responses are often 
more affected than the sural responses. Because CIDP is multifo-
cal and affects motor nerves and proximal segments predomi -
nantly, sural biopsy can be normal and therefore is not routinely 
done. However, sural nerve biopsy, when affected, shows demye-
lination or findings pointing to other disorders that can mimic 
CIDP , such as vasculitis or amyloidosis. As in AIDP , a lumbar 
puncture will show albuminocytologic dissociation in 90% of 
patients. MRI with gadolinium of the brachial and lumbar plex -
uses and cauda equina can reveal enhancing or enlarged nerves.
The European Academy of Neurology/Peripheral Nerve Soci -
ety proposed diagnostic criteria for “typical” CIDP and “CIDP 
variants” based on review of available evidence and expert consen-
sus.177 Diagnostic criteria include both clinical ( Box 42.3 ) and 
electrodiagnostic components, which can be used to identify 
probable or possible disease. CIDP and CIDP variants are diag -
nosed based on the presence of clinical features, motor and sen -
sory nerve conduction criteria, and supportive features.
The 2021 consensus criteria 177 should be referenced to guide 
electrophysiologic workup and diagnostic impression. Broadly, 
the threshold of abnormality to establish systemic demyelinating 
disease exceeds that of identifying a single demyelinating feature 
that could be explained by mononeuropathy alone. In particular, 
diagnosis of CIDP requires demyelinating range findings across 
multiple nerves, not exclusively at common entrapment sites (e.g., 
median nerve at the wrist) and not inclusive of conduction blocks 
in segments that often exhibit such findings (e.g., tibial nerve in 
the leg). Motor electrodiagnostic criteria for CIDP include the 
following in at least two nerves:
• Motor distal latency prolongation $50% of the upper limit of 
normal
• CV ,30% of the lower limit of normal

Similar to GBS, CIDP is responsive to IVIG and PE. CIDP 
also responds to corticosteroids, but GBS does not. IVIG and PE 
can be used to bring rapid improvement in symptoms in severe 
attacks; however, they rarely result in complete remission, and 
most patients require repeated treatment at intervals of 2 to 6 weeks 
to prevent relapse.
94 IVIG is usually favored over PE. Corticoste -
roids are more likely than IVIG or PE to produce remission of 
disease in the more common demyelinating form; however, corti-
costeroids are not effective and can even be detrimental to pure 
motor variants of CIDP . Metanalysis showed that the children 
have an 80% to 100% response rate to standard treatments of 
IVIG and corticosteroids, and complete functional recovery is 
seen in most children.
103 After stabilization of initial onset or re -
currence of CIDP , appropriate physical and occupational therapy, 
orthotic management, and treatment of neuropathic pain should 
be addressed. A course of inpatient rehabilitation might be ap -
propriate after severe episodes.

cant problem in several countries. HIV is responsible for a num -
ber of neuropathic syndromes, and the agents used to treat HIV 
can also be responsible for polyneuropathy. Chronic hepatitis C 
viral infection is a growing global health issue that is associated 
with polyneuropathy. Lyme disease, a tickborne infection com-
mon in the United States and Europe, is also responsible for 
polyneuropathy. Infection can cause neuropathy either through 
the inflammatory reactions induced by the infectious agent or 
through the body’s immune reaction to the organism.
Leprosy (Hansen Disease)
Leprosy is caused by Mycobacterium leprae, which is found pri-
marily in tropical and subtropical developing countries. There are 
three clinical presentations, which are determined by the host 
immune response to the bacillus: tuberculoid, lepromatous, and 
borderline leprosy. T uberculoid leprosy causes single or multiple 
well-circumscribed cutaneous lesions. High levels of cell-mediated 
immunity kill the bacilli and cause focal destruction of the 
nerves that travel through the granulomatous tuberculoid  
lesions. The most commonly involved nerves are the ulnar, me-
dian, common fibular, facial, superficial radial, digital, posterior 
auricular, and sural nerves.
141 The bacillus tends to proliferate in 
parts of the body with areas of lower temperature. The leproma -
tous form occurs when there is deficient cell-mediated immune 
response. No granulomas are formed, and nerve destruction 
occurs from direct nerve invasion of the bacillus, particularly 
affecting the Schwann cell. Nerve destruction is typically diffuse 
and symmetric. In both forms, the predominant finding is sen -
sory loss affecting nerves in areas of lower temperature such as 
extensor surfaces, earlobes, and over the maxilla. The soles of the 
feet and the palms are typically spared. Motor weakness devel -
ops late and only after there is significant sensory loss. Leprosy 
is treated with dapsone, which itself can cause neuropathy.  
Dapsone primarily causes a progressive motor neuropathy that 
can preferentially affect the hip girdle muscles or both hand and 
foot muscles in a symmetric fashion.
Lyme Disease
Lyme disease is a tickborne illness that is caused by the spirochete 
Borrelia burgdorferi. There are three stages of disease: early infec -
tion that characterized by a local erythematous lesion (erythema 
migrans), disseminated infection, and late-stage infection. Men -
ingitis is the most common neurologic manifestation in Lyme 
disease. Peripheral nerve abnormalities vary with the stage of the 
disease. Cranial mononeuropathies can be seen in the second 
stage, affecting the facial nerve in up to 80%.
160 Radiculoneuritis 
also is reported that progresses to a mononeuritis multiplex pat -
tern (i.e., plexopathy). The lower limbs are affected more than 
the upper limbs. Although much less common now because of 
better detection, in the late stage of untreated Lyme disease about 
half of the patients develop a distal symmetric neuropathy, pre -
senting with a stocking glove distribution of paresthesia and 
sensory loss. Other presentations can also occur, including 
mononeuropathies, acute polyradiculoneuropathies, and asym-
metric distal neuropathies. Although the clinical presentation of 
peripheral nerve dysfunction is broad, the underlying injury is an 
asymmetric axonal neuropathy. T reatment of Lyme disease with 
neurologic involvement consists of intravenous ceftriaxone for  
2 to 4 weeks. Improvement in acute neurologic symptoms is 
fairly good with antibiotic treatment; however, chronic neuropa -
thies respond more slowly and can result in residual deficits.

HIV-Associated Neuropathies
HIV infection is associated with a range of neurologic disorders 
that can occur at any stage of the disease. Early in HIV infection, 
these neurologic insults are caused by immune dysregulation. 
AIDP can occur at the time of seroconversion, whereas CIDP mani-
fests later, usually when CD4 counts are less than 50 cells/mm.50 The 
clinical features are identical to those in HIV-negative patients and 
are treated in the same manner and respond to treatment similarly 
to patients without HIV . Immune-mediated vasculitic neuropa -
thies, cranial mononeuropathies, brachial plexopathies, and mul -
tiple mononeuropathies are also seen early in HIV infection. In 
mid- to late-stage infection, neuropathies are usually related to the 
HIV replication, most commonly a sensory polyneuropathy or an 
autonomic neuropathy. Later-stage disease brings neuropathies  
associated with opportunistic infection, such as CMV , syphilis, 
and zoster. Neuropathies associated with malignancy and nutri -
tional deficiency are also seen. Drugs used to treat HIV infection 
are also known to cause neuropathy. As an example, nucleoside 
reverse transcriptase inhibitors cause a distal sensory neuropathy 
that is clinically and electrodiagnostically identical to that associ -
ated with primary HIV infection. The use of antiretroviral therapy 
(ART) can also lead to exaggerated immune response to an already 
present opportunistic infection such as M. leprae. This is known as 
the immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) and is 
associated with a CD4 count lower than 50 cells/mL at the initia-
tion of ART . IRIS is associated with greater morbidity and mortal-
ity and is usually managed by the discontinuation of ART and the 
use of corticosteroids or thalidomide. 99 Diffuse infiltrative lym -
phocytosis syndrome (DILS) can be seen in HIV-positive patients 
with a persistent CD8 hyperlymphocytosis and Sjögren-like syn -
drome. In these individuals, nerve biopsy will show CD8 1 infil-
tration causing a painful sensorimotor axonal neuropathy that re-
sponds to ART and steroids. It is believed to be an exaggerated 
immune response to HIV (Box 42.4).
A symmetric distal sensory polyneuropathy can be seen ei -
ther because of HIV distal sensory polyneuropathy (HIV-DSP) 
or antiretroviral drug toxicity distal sensory polyneuropathy 
(ARV-DSP). Since the introduction of highly active ARV treat -
ment, the incidence of clinical DSP is estimated to be 30%, 
although on autopsy close to 100% of patients demonstrated 
histologic evidence of DSP . 68 Risk factors for HIV-DSP are 
older age, a CD4 count less than 50 cells/mm 3, coexisting dia -
betes mellitus or nutritional deficiency, and use of neurotoxic 
drugs and alcohol. 27 DSP causes distal axonal degeneration of 
small unmyelinated fibers. Pain and paresthesias progress in a 
symmetric “stocking glove” distribution. Proprioception and 
strength are relatively preserved. Achilles reflexes are absent or 
decreased. Electrodiagnostic studies, which are relatively insen -
sitive to small fiber dysfunction, might be normal or demon -
strate a length-dependent sensory axonal neuropathy. Needle 
EMG examination is typically normal, although it occasionally 
can show chronic denervation and reinnervation in foot intrin -
sic muscles. Skin biopsy with epidermal nerve fiber density will 
detect a small fiber neuropathy and correlates with severity of 
painful symptoms. 154 It is important to differentiate between 
HIV-DSP and ARV-DSP because discontinuation of the drug 
can reverse ARV-DSP . Differentiation is usually made on his -
torical grounds based on a close temporal relationship between 
drug initiation and the onset of symptoms. Discontinuation  
or reduction of dose leads to improvement within 8 weeks,  
although there can be an initial period of worsening after stop -
ping the drug. 53
Typical distal symmetric polyneuropathy is more common in 
adult patients with HIV-1 infection than in children. Polyneu -
ropathy in children with HIV has not been extensively studied 
but occurs often in the form of AIDP at the time of seroconver -
sion as an immune reconstitution phenomenon and in relation to 
secondary infection with CMV .190
CMV infection in HIV can cause a severe, rapidly progressive 
polyradiculomyelopathy, which is a true neurologic emergency. It 
presents with symptoms of cauda equina syndrome developing 
over days to weeks. It initially presents with low back pain and 
unilateral lower limb radiation with urinary incontinence, fol -
lowed quickly by progressive leg weakness and saddle anesthesia. 
If untreated, flaccid paraplegia develops with death within weeks. 
CSF reveals CMV infection by culture, polymerase chain reaction 
assay, elevated protein, low glucose, and polymorphonuclear pleo-
cytosis. Electrodiagnostic studies are consistent with an axonal 
loss involving the lumbosacral roots. MRI may show enhance -
ment of the lumbosacral roots. CMV-associated polyradiculoneu-
ropathy is treated with ganciclovir or other antiviral agents. A 
similar clinical picture can be seen in syphilitic, tubercular, or 
cryptococcal infection, lymphoma, and concurrent HTLV1 infec-
tion as well as in IRIS and DILS. 50

Hepatitis C virus (HCV) affects 2% to 3% of the world’s popula-
tion,91 and polyneuropathy is the most common neurologic 
complication. Approximately 15% of those with HCV will have 
polyneuropathy detected on standard nerve conduction studies, 
although one-third have subclinical disease. 142 Small fiber neu -
ropathy has a prevalence of over 40% in the HCV-infected

Toxic Neuropathies
Although toxins are a less common cause of polyneuropathy, 
prompt diagnosis and identification of toxic insult can lead to 
improvement in withdrawal of the offending agent. Recognition 
of a source of chronic exposure in the household or workplace 
can also protect others at risk. T oxic exposure can be (1) occupa-
tional, as occurs with industrial solvents and glues; (2) environ -
mental, as in lead exposure; (3) secondary to abuse, as seen  
in alcohol abuse or the “huffing” of household solvents; (4) the 
result of intentional poisoning (suicide or homicide attempts); or 
(5) iatrogenic from medications used to treat a variety of diseases 
(Table 42.10).
As with any neuropathy evaluation, a careful exposure history 
should be obtained, which includes understanding the patient’s 
workplace and any chemicals there that may lead to exposure. 
Recreational activities are another source of exposure, particularly 
ones that involve glues or solvents, or exposure to pesticides. In the 
case of children, especially those with pica, exposure to toys con -
taining lead-based paint may be a particular hazard. A recreational 
drug history may reveal heavy alcohol use or intentional inhalation 
of glues and aerosols (huffing). T oxic neuropathies frequently pres-
ent with other systemic effects, ranging from subtle to severe and 
life threatening. Gastrointestinal complaints are seen in arsenic, 
lead, and thallium poisoning. Cutaneous findings can be seen, 
such as Mees lines in the fingernails in lead and arsenic poisoning; 
gingival abnormalities in phenytoin, lead, and mercury exposure; 
and irritant dermatitis with acrylamide, thallium, and toluene  
exposure.
95 Cardiovascular, hepatic, and renal abnormalities  
are associated with many toxic exposures. Exposure to organo -
phosphates causes symptoms of cholinergic excess, including 
bradycardia, sweating, excess salivation, miosis, bronchospasm, 
and diarrhea. The CNS and the peripheral nervous system can be 
affected. Chronic alcohol abuse can cause midline cerebellar de -
generation and a triad of ataxia, dementia, and ophthalmoplegia

169 The developing brain is particularly

The patient’s medication list should always be carefully reviewed 
because a number of pharmaceutic agents can cause nerve toxicity. 
Many factors can increase the risk of medication-induced polyneu-
ropathy, including impaired renal or hepatic function causing 
decreased drug metabolism or clearance and the presence of an 
unrelated neuropathy such as Charcot-Marie-T ooth (CMT) disease.
Chemotherapy-induced polyneuropathy is a very important 
source of morbidity in cancer patients. Not only does it impact 
quality of life, but also it can limit treatment. Clinical features 
will vary with the specific chemotherapeutic agent. Taxanes typi -
cally cause a bilateral symmetric axonal neuropathy that is sen -
sory predominant.
145 Incidence and severity depend on both 
dose per cycle and the total cumulative dose received. Other risk 
factors include rate of infusion, use of other neurotoxic drugs, 
and comorbidities such as diabetes or CMT . Although mild 
symptoms improve with dose reduction, mild sensory symptoms 
may persist in 14% of patients after cessation of treatment  
taxels.
116 Vinca alkaloids, particularly vincristine, cause some 
decrease of a mixed sensorimotor neuropathy in almost all treated 
patients.124 Autonomic neuropathies are common, and mononeu-
ropathies may occur, most commonly affecting the oculomotor 
nerve.
8 Platinum-containing agents primarily affect the dorsal  
root ganglion. Cisplatin-induced polyneuropathy predominantly 
affects large sensory fibers. Oxaliplatin causes an acute, reversible 
neuropathy and a chronic polyneuropathy characterized by distal 
symmetric axonal sensory involvement.
187 Symptoms of neuropa-
thy can appear or progress for up to 2 months after cessation of the 
chemotherapeutic agent, known as the “coasting phenomenon.”
57 
Dose reduction, longer infusion times, or longer periods between 
doses may improve symptoms, but sometimes treatment must be 
completely stopped, and recovery can sometimes be incomplete.
Vasculitic and Connective Tissue  
Disease–Associated Neuropathies
Vasculitis and connective tissue diseases produce neuropathies 
with varied presentations. Distal symmetric neuropathy and sin -
gle mononeuropathies do occur, but the prototypical neuropathy 
caused by vasculitic and connective tissue disorders is an asym -
metric mixed motor and sensory pattern referred to as a mononeu-
ritis multiplex. The classic presentation begins with vasculitic 
ischemia and acute onset of pain and sensory abnormality in the 
distribution of the affected nerve. Occasionally the symptoms 
progress in a more gradual fashion, although it is typically asym -
metric. Lower limb nerves are more commonly affected. The 
cranial nerves are rarely affected. The nerves affected are more 
likely to have injury at more proximal sites than those seen with 
typical compression neuropathies.
Vasculitic disorders that commonly cause polyneuropathy in-
clude polyarteritis nodosa, Churg-Strauss syndrome, microscopic 
polyangiitis, and Wegener granulomatosis (Box 42.5
). Nerves can 
also be affected by nonsystemic vasculitis of the peripheral nerves 
(i.e., a vasculitis without other organ or system involvement). The 
vasculitic injury to the peripheral nervous system usually results 
in the asymmetric mononeuritis multiplex pattern. Connective 
tissue disorders, such as RA, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), 
and Sjögren syndrome, more often cause a diffuse symmetric 
polyneuropathy.
RA and SLE tend to cause a polyneuropathy that is mild and a 
late presentation in the disease. RA can progress to rheumatoid 
vasculitis, causing multiple mononeuropathies. Compression neu-
ropathies, especially CTS, are more common in both SLE and RA. 
Sjögren syndrome can also present as a pure sensory neuropathy.
The hallmark of mononeuritis multiplex on physical examina-
tion is weakness or sensory loss that represents a side-to-side 
asymmetry of the same peripheral nerve or proximal greater than 
distal involvement of different peripheral nerves in sites that are 
not typical locations of anatomic compression. EDX in mono -
neuritis multiplex shows decreased SNAP amplitudes in affected 
nerves, with normal to borderline low CV . The findings are 
similar for the motor conductions. Marked asymmetry between 
the same nerves on the right and left sides may be seen. Nerves 
in the lower extremities are usually more affected. Conduction 
block can be seen in the affected nerves, in segments not typically 
involved in compression. F-waves can be prolonged. Needle 
EMG shows positive waves and fibrillation potentials with 
reduced recruitment and neuropathic motor unit changes in the 
territory of the affected nerves. As the disease progresses and in-
volves more nerves, the findings can coalesce, making differentia-
tion from a diffuse, symmetric pattern difficult. In patients with 
known vasculitic or connective tissue disease, the findings of new

Hereditary Neuropathies
Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease
CMT disease is the most common inherited polyneuropathy with 
a prevalence of 17 to 40 per 100,000.
102 CMT is a genetically 
heterogeneous disorder characterized by wasting of the distal limb 
muscles, particularly the fibular innervated muscles, with distal 
sensory loss, decreased or absent muscle stretch reflexes, and skel-
etal deformity. CMT is often referred to as hereditary motor and 
sensory neuropathy (HMSN) and has been subclassified as 
HMSN types I through VI. HMSN-I, now known as CMT-1, is 
characterized by slow CVs and diffuse demyelination. HMSN-II/
CMT-2 is the axonal form with axonal degeneration and pre-
served or only mildly slowed CVs. HMSN-III is more commonly 
known as Déjèrine-Sottas neuropathy and is characterized by 
early onset with severe hypertrophic demyelination. HMSN-IV , 
HMSN-V , and HMSN-VI describe various motor-only forms 
now referred to as distal hereditary motor neuropathies. At least  
40 different genetic forms of CMT have been identified, and any 
one genotype can have variable penetrance and phenotypical ex -
pression.
76
CMT typically shows a pattern of slow progression of onset, 
starting in the first 2 decades of life. Children and teenagers  
can first present with steppage gait caused by weakness of the  
fibular innervated muscles and the development of pes cavus  
(Fig. 42.23).184 Weakness of the ankle plantar flexors is less severe, 
and atrophy of the lower limbs is often described as resembling an 
inverted champagne bottle. Upper limb involvement with claw 
hand deformity occurs later in the course of the disease. Although 
gait is affected, loss of independent ambulation is rare.
63 Sensory 
symptoms are less prominent. Loss of sensation for pain, touch, and 
vibration is seen in the feet, with clinical proprioception relatively 
preserved. Pain or paresthesias are less common and less severe, but 
complaints of cold feet and muscle cramps may be noted. Muscle 
stretch reflexes are decreased to absent in the demyelinating CMT-1 
but are less affected in CMT-2. Skeletal deformities are seen in 66% 
of all CMT patients, with pes cavus and hammer toes the most 
common abnormality. Scoliosis is less common. Foot deformities 
occur as a result of the imbalance in strength of the foot intrinsic 
and leg muscles. Late-onset CMT tends to result in subtle foot 
abnormalities if at all.
120
Nerve conduction tests are the primary diagnostic tests for 
CMT . CMT-1, the demyelinating form of CMT , is characterized 
by marked slowing of nerve conduction studies, defined as me -
dian nerve conduction values less than 38 m/sec.
63 Discriminat-
ing hereditary from acquired neuropathies can be challenging

There is a growing body of evidence demonstrating the merits 
of exercise for people with CMT and showing that not only will it 
not hurt, but it actually has the potential to improve functional 
ability, independence, and quality of life. Because function is likely 
highest in children with CMT as compared with adults, regular 
exercise and activity may help preserve physical ability into adult-
hood. Progressive strengthening of the hip flexors improves hip 
flexor fatigue, resulting in improved walking duration, and 
strengthening of the ankle musculature results in improved muscle 
strength, walking speed, cadence, step time, and stride length. 
Children, like adults with CMT , have shown responsiveness to 
progressive strength training with improvement in muscle strength 
and walking speed, cadence, step time, and stride length.17,129
Autonomic Neuropathy
Autonomic neuropathy, often associated with diabetes, is a sig -
nificant cause of morbidity and even mortality, affecting the car -
diovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, and thermoregulatory 
systems. Involvement of the cardiovascular system limits activity 
tolerance and possibly life-threatening consequences. Patients 
with cardiac autonomic neuropathy have 40% greater mortality 
compared with those without, 78 which is caused by silent isch -
emia, lethal arrhythmias, or prolonged QT interval. Those with 
cardiac involvement have resting tachycardia greater than 100 
beats/min and orthostasis, a decrease of systolic blood pressure 
greater than 20 mmHg on standing, which occurs without ap -
propriate heart rate response. This leads to exercise tolerance and 
increases the risk of sudden death and silent myocardial ischemia. 
Both hypotension and hypertension can develop after exercise. 
Orthostasis is the most common clinical complaint. Management 
includes education regarding the need for slow postural changes, 
elevation of the head of the bed, and use of compression gar -
ments, such as compressive stockings and abdominal binders. If 
these measures are inadequate, mineralocorticoids, such as fludro-
cortisone or the alpha agonist midodrine, can be used.
Autonomic dysfunction of the gastrointestinal system can lead 
to esophageal dysmotility, gastroparesis, constipation or diarrhea, 
and bowel incontinence. Gastroparesis causes a sensation of bloat-
ing, nausea, heartburn, early satiety, and erratic glycemic control. 
These symptoms are typically managed with metoclopramide.
Autonomic neuropathy can lead to irreversible erectile dys -
function.43 T reatment consists of assessing for secondary causes of 
erectile dysfunction, counseling, medications, suction erectile 
devices, and penile implants. Neurogenic bladder leads to overflow 
incontinence, recurrent urinary tract infection, and pyelonephritis. 
Urinary retention can be diagnosed with postvoid residuals and 
might need more formal urodynamic studies to determine the 
severity. In mild cases, the patient can be instructed to void every 
2 to 3 hours while awake. In more severe cases, intermittent cath-
eterization may be necessary.
In those with abnormalities in sweating and thermoregulation, 
there should be counseling about adequate hydration and caution 
with exertion in hot or cold environments. Lack of sweating can 
lead to dryness and cracking and fissuring of the feet, increasing 
susceptibility to foot ulceration and infection. Patients should be 
taught to keep their feet well moisturized.
Complications of Neuropathies
Foot Complications
Foot ulceration is a major and serious complication in patients 
with polyneuropathy, particularly in those with diabetes mellitus, 
who manifest a lifetime risk of 15%. 49 The presence of neuropa-
thy is associated with an 8- to 18-fold increased risk of foot ulcer 
and a 2- to 15-fold greater risk of amputation. 118 Loss of protec-
tive sensation in polyneuropathy may lead to unrecognized foot 
trauma and skin breakdown. Motor neuropathy causes muscle 
atrophy and weakness, distorting normal foot architecture and 
leading to abnormal pressure distribution and overloaded plantar 
areas, further increasing risk of ulceration.
Autonomic dysfunction further compromises skin integrity by 
causing decreased sweating, resulting in skin dryness and crack -
ing. The detrimental impact of autonomic dysfunction is accentu-
ated by arteriovenous shunting, leading to alteration of perfusion 
with secondary osteopenia caused by bone resorption and im -
paired healing. Neuropathy can also lead to Charcot changes. 
Charcot neuroarthropathy is characterized by destruction of the 
joints of the foot, with pathologic fracture and joint dislocation 
usually in the midfoot region, which results in severe foot defor -
mity (Fig. 42.24).73
Patients with neuropathy should be educated on the impor -
tance of daily inspection of their feet. Protective socks and ap -
propriate shoe wear, including extra-depth toe boxes and custom 
orthoses, should be used to offload high-pressure areas. Feet 
should be kept dry and the skin well moisturized, and extreme 
temperatures should be avoided, particularly hot water. Nail care 
and callus management is important and may require regular care 
by a podiatrist or diabetic foot clinic. Prompt management of any 
skin breakdown and aggressive management of infection are im -
portant to avoid progression to amputation. Acute Charcot foot 
can be difficult to differentiate from cellulitis, presenting with a 
warm, erythematous, and swollen foot. Radiographs may be nor -
mal in the acute phase. Fractures and joint dislocation will be -
come apparent later in the course of the disease; however, a bone 
scan is often positive early on. Strict immobilization with a total 
contact cast is used to manage acute Charcot arthropathy and 
maintained until clinical signs of warmth and tenderness have 
significantly decreased and the bone scan becomes negative or less 
active, indicating that bone remodeling is near complete. At this 
point, careful weaning from the orthosis can begin. This is often 
accompanied by physical therapy using a gradual resumption of 
weight bearing and strengthening foot and ankle musculature. 
Prevention and treatment programs can significantly reduce lower 
extremity breakdowns and amputations.
Pain
Neuropathic pain is common and can be quite incapacitating. 
Large fiber neuropathies often manifest with dull, deep, tooth -
ache-like, or cramping pain. Small fiber neuropathies affect small

autonomic function and initially manifest with significant pain in 
the feet. Small fiber neuropathy is typically perceived as superfi -
cial, burning, and hypersensitive. Other sources of foot pain 
should be excluded. Other causes of painful feet are lumbar  
radiculopathy, vascular claudication, tarsal tunnel syndrome,

also be kept in mind that each of these diagnoses can coexist with 
symptomatic painful neuropathy.
T reatment of neuropathic pain can be challenging, and the 
side effects of the medications can be particularly problematic in 
elderly patients. T opical agents, such as capsaicin cream, have the 
advantage of having no systemic side effects, but the application 
can be cumbersome (requiring application three to four times per 
day) and can cause initial irritation. T ransdermal lidocaine 
patches can be useful in those with a relatively discrete distribu -
tion of pain and are easier to apply. T ranscutaneous electrical 
stimulation may also be beneficial. Oral neuropathic pain medi-
cations, including gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, and 
duloxetine, have been shown to be effective in treatment of 
neuropathic pain. It is important to consider the side effect pro -
file of each class when starting a medication for treatment of 
neuropathic pain. T ricyclic antidepressant dosing is often limited 
by anticholinergic effects, which include sedation, dry mouth, 
urinary retention, orthostatic hypotension, and potentially  
dangerous arrhythmias. Low-dose nortriptyline, dosed at 10 to  
50 mg at bedtime, may be better tolerated than amitriptyline.  
It has been shown to have equivalent effect on some types of 
neuropathic pain,
185 with fewer side effects. Gabapentin and 
pregabalin can cause somnolence and dizziness, particularly on 
initiation of treatment, and they can exacerbate gait and cogni -
tive problems in older patients. Use of duloxetine can be limited 
by gastrointestinal side effects. T ramadol, a weak mu-opioid  
receptor agonist and a weak norepinephrine and serotonin reup -
take inhibitor, has been shown to have beneficial effects on the 
allodynia associated with diabetic polyneuropathy.
155
T ramadol can cause cognitive impairment, and serotonin syn-
drome may occur with concomitant use of other serotonergic 
medications. Opioid analgesics have been shown to improve pain 
and sleep but not function or mood.
128 Because opioids can cause 
cognitive side effects, physiologic dependency, and addiction, 
they should be used with extreme caution. Second-line medica -
tions for the treatment of neuropathic pain include lamotrigine, 
carbamazepine, other selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and 
clonidine.
Functional Impairment
Polyneuropathy can significantly affect functional mobility and 
quality of life. Patients with polyneuropathy are about 20 times 
more likely to fall than those without neuropathy.
132,136 Neuropa-
thy impairs proprioceptive sensation at the ankle, and those with 
neuropathy are less able to rapidly develop the ankle torque 
needed to correct for lateral lean.
58 Patients with neuropathy are 
additionally penalized when their center of mass is displaced as a 
relatively greater displacement occurs before loss of balance is 
perceived, and then there is a delay in corrective ankle torque 
generation. As a result, the patient with neuropathy experiences 
dynamic postural instability, particularly on uneven surfaces and 
in dim lighting, where visual cues cannot assist in compensating 
for proprioceptive loss. Both the perceived and real risk of insta -
bility can lead to increasingly limited physical activity, physical 
deconditioning, isolation, and depression.
182
Although neuropathy-related impairments in ankle sensory and 
motor function have been recognized for some time, an under -
standing of the relationship between poor distal neuromuscular 
function and proximal strength has only recently developed. It has 
been recognized that the ratio of proximal strength at the hip to 
ankle proprioceptive precision predicts unipedal stance time,
3 falls, 
and fall-related injury in older patients with diabetic neuropa -
thy.133 The clinical relevance of the ratio suggests that increased hip 
strength can compensate for imprecise ankle proprioceptive preci-
sion. Of note, patients with neuropathy caused by type 2 diabetes

mellitus respond well to resistance training.125 There are a number 
of other interventions that can improve gait and balance. Vision 
should be regularly tested, and corrective eyewear should be used as 
needed. Patients should consider using glasses for walking that cor-
rect distance vision only because the use of bifocals has been inde-
pendently associated with falls. 96 Proper lighting of the patient’s 
environment is important, particularly when getting up at night. 
Proper shoe wear that has a wide base of support and thin soles can 
improve balance. A cane can stabilize gait, but to achieve maximal 
benefit, patients should be able to support up to 25% of their body 
weight on the cane to prevent a fall.6 The cane should be brought 
down with each contralateral footstep. Acceptance of the use of a 
cane can be improved if presented as a substitute for the loss of a 
special sense, much like eyeglasses, and used only as needed on 
uneven surfaces and in unfamiliar areas. However, a disadvantage of 
canes is that they are associated with decreased walking speed. Use 
of ankle orthoses with medial and lateral support has been shown 
to improve gait and stability parameters.138 They also have the ad-
vantage of freeing up both hands and better walking speed than a 
cane, but the skin in contact with the orthoses must be monitored 
for breakdown. Exercise trials involving neuropathic older subjects 
demonstrated that tai chi and functional balance training have 
shown improvement in clinical measurements of balance in patients 
with neuropathy.127,137 Specific exercises aimed at strengthening hip 
abductors and adductors, as well as abdominals, can improve fron-
tal plane hip and trunk stability. Strengthening of grip, shoulder 
depression, and elbow extension can improve support when using 
a cane or other means of upper limb stabilization.
